responseSet.title: Paola Marion – Il disagio del desiderio by Paola Marion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Paola Marion – Il disagio del desiderio* by Paola Marion*

Benedetta Degl’Innocenti

Benedetta Degl’Innocenti

Firenze
Italy
guerrinib@gmail.com

Benedetta Degl’Innocenti  

Paola Marion’s book Il disagio del desiderio addresses, in the context of psychoanalytic reflection and practice, the potential repercussions of biotechnological innovations in the field of human procreation on founding aspects of personal identity such as the relationship with the body, sexuality, and the domain of pleasure. There are many features which make this study remarkable. First of all, the author has made an impressive effort to hold together the problematic aspects of a technology that is still evolving and its repercussions, as inevitable as they are unforeseeable, on the psychic life of individuals and couples, at the same time re-elaborating the Freudian legacy in the light of almost a century of often mutually challenging theories.

The whole of the book’s first part (“The scandal of sexuality”) is devoted to retracing the concept of the sexual and sexuality in psychoanalytic thought, illuminating consonances and dissonances between the models from Freud onwards, in order to describe – as the author says – what sexuality has meant for psychoanalysis, but above all what psychoanalysis has meant for sexuality. The author does this by following the lead of two articles which proposed two different theoretical models and have been important re-openings of the debate about the subject after a long period of the so-called “desexualisation” of psychoanalysis: André Green’s “Has sexuality anything to do with psychoanalysis?” from 1995, and the article published by Peter Fonagy in the International Journal in 2008, entitled “A genuinely developmental theory of sexual enjoyment and its implications for psychoanalytic technique”. Following these writers’ reflections, Marion highlights how, at a certain point, the advance of object relations theory and the predominance of attention on the early mother-child relationship eclipsed genital sexuality and the Oedipus complex in importance, with the risk – which Marion also emphasises – of setting up a collusive denial of their significance within the analytic relationship. Starting with these considerations, the author accompanies the reader through the rediscovery of human sexuality as a core concept, alongside the unconscious and the dream, of this anthropology of modern times which is psychoanalysis: sexuality as the foundation of the human, not reducible merely to its biological and instinctual level, but the meeting point par excellence between body and mind, self and other.

responseSet.title: The history of countertransference: by Alberto Stefana, London and New York, Routledge, 2017,159 pp.paperback, £33.99, ISBN 978-1-138-21461-3
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The history of countertransference

by Alberto Stefana, London and New York, Routledge, 2017,159 pp.paperback, £33.99, ISBN 978-1-138-21461-3

Ricardo Stramer

Ricardo Stramer

London
UK

Ricardo Stramer   Ricardostramer12@gmail.com

This is primarily a psycho-historical book that aims to study the theoretical conceptualisation and clinical use of counter-transference as it has evolved over the years. The author is an experienced psychoanalytic psychotherapist and academic, with a background in clinical psychology, who has written extensively on the history and epistemology of psychoanalysis. Following a foreword by Robert Hinshelwood, in which he suggests that this book attempts to bring some much-needed cohesion to the various theoretical strands on the subject of counter-transference, the author suggests that the great spread in the understanding of the principles involved in the identification, definition and utilisation of counter-transference emerged following the various pathologies encountered by analysts in practice.

The first chapter, entitled “The Origins of the Notion of Counter-transference,” discusses the early struggles of the psychoanalytic pioneers. The first time a public debate occurred on the phenomenon of counter-transference was in 1910 at the 2nd IPA Congress held in Nuremberg. Freud then spoke of the need for the psychoanalyst to exercise the task of self-analysis, in response to the patient’s transferential impact on him. Stefana thinks that Freud had a “bi-forkal” vision of counter-transference: on one hand, it is something to be suppressed, and on the other, a useful tool to perceive the unconscious communication transmitted by the patient.

Interestingly, the author suggests that Freud’s resistance to using counter-transference as an interactive instrument had a political and protective motive due to the fact that his contemporaries were struggling with it and generating their own theories. At the same time, the author suggests that Freud was fully conscious of the unconscious power of the effect of the transference and the value of the analyst’s use of the counter-transference mainly not to interfere with the patient’s unconscious communications.

Chapter 2, “Freud and the Psychoanalytic Movement between the Foundation of the IPA, the Great War and the Turning Point of 1920,” is fairly succinct yet very significant as it covers the Klein–Anna Freud controversies on the origins of phantasy life in children and the corresponding transference development in children.

[[RunningHead]]

Contributors and Reviewers for 2019

The Editors, Dana Birksted-Breen, Francis Grier, Jorge Canestri, Lucy LaFarge, Beatriz de León de Bernardi, Georg Bruns, and Catherine Humble would like to express their gratitude to the team of readers and reviewers throughout the world responsible for the successful implementation of the Journal’s peer-review process. These colleagues devote a great deal of time and effort in the service of providing detailed and thoughtful evaluations of manuscripts, film essays and book reviews which will ultimately help the authors strengthen their contributions. We would also like to thank the section editors for all their hard work: Heinz Weiss (Education Section), Rachel Blass (Psychoanalytic Controversies), Abbot Bronstein (Analyst at Work), Caroline Bainbridge (Film Essays), Maria Cristina Garcia Vasconcellos (Executive Editor, IJP-es), Gustavo Jarast (Associate Editor IJP-es), Emily Alster and Anne Patterson, (Book Reviews), Bruce Reis (North American Book Reviews), and all our Book Review Associates. Without the dedication of section editors, reviewers, and readers, the IJP would not have achieved the scholarly status it enjoys, and we would like to thank them for everything they have done to assist us during the past 12 months. In addition, we would like to thank the board members and editors of the IJP Annuals who produce collections of IJP papers in various languages. We wish to express our appreciation to our translators who carry out essential work for the Journal’s aims. Last but not least, we would like to thank Chris Lawrence our Journal Assistant for his hard work behind the scenes.

responseSet.title: Chaos and control: a psychoanalytic perspective on unfolding creative minds, by Desy Safán-Gerard
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Chaos and control: a psychoanalytic perspective on unfolding creative minds, by Desy Safán-Gerard

Mark Mellinger

Mark Mellinger

New York
USA

Mark Mellinger   markmellinger680@gmail.com

Desy Safán-Gerard is a psychoanalyst and a painter and her book is replete with colour plates of her paintings, which illustrate her approach to working creatively. The images also help the 408 reader to get a deep sense of who the author is and how she approaches a problem. The text is equally revealing and undefended. Safán-Gerard shares personal experiences, thoughts and feelings in a remarkably open way. As a psychoanalyst and artist, I wondered which part of my two lives this book would speak to. It turns out that it was the artist that she spoke to most eloquently. This is in part because making art is an attempt to communicate silently, and it is unusual to read such a skilful verbalization of that unspoken process. Reading Chaos and Control, I identified with my artist self and thought about the challenges and failures in my own work.

There were elements of the text, however, which returned me to my analyst self. Safán-Gerard makes use of an eclectic mix of theory in her analytic work and in her understanding of the creative process. She is centred around a Klein/Bion model but is in no way rejecting of Freudian and relational tools for understanding creativity. I confess to some difficulty with Kleinian thinking. The “this means that” aspect makes for fascinating speculation and for a mystery novel thrill, but strikes me as reifying in a way that is contrary to the wordlessness of visual art. Safán-Gerard’s existential approach to Bion’s “O” seems to me a more useful way to integrate art and psychoanalysis. I find her use of Kleinian interpretations to interrupt the continuity of her imaginative integration. Others may well have a different experience.

One theme she takes up concerns the independence of the artwork from the artist. She cites Marion Milner’s focus on the artist’s dialogue with the external world via the artwork. Though she doesn’t specifically cite the concept of the Third, Safán-Gerard sees artistic creation in those terms. Once begun, the artwork begins to dictate its will to the artist. While avoiding sinking into mystification, Safán-Gerard describes the function of the artist as not exactly in control of her creation. Indeed, submission to the chaotic primary process impulses is necessary to avoid making art that is essentially a narcissistic expression of conscious intentions. If the work is done “to” the painting and not “with” the painting, she sees the work as narcissistic. The artist must continuously destroy and recreate her work in order to free herself from the conventional and the predictable.

responseSet.title: An infant’s contribution towards the joyful feminine in perinatal intervention
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An infant’s contribution towards the joyful feminine in perinatal intervention

Frances Thomson-Salo

Frances Thomson-Salo

Australia

Frances Thomson-Salo   CONTACT Frances Thomson-Salo fvtsalo@optusnet.com.au PO Box, 1184, Windsor, Victoria, Australia

I will make two main points, firstly, to consider a baby’s contribution to the feminine within Western psychoanalytic notions, trying for a major alignment of psychoanalysis with clinical infant mental health, to bridge these perspectives with those from infant studies (and neuroscience), to link psychoanalysis with nearby sciences and to illustrate an intersubjective approach in brief perinatal infant–parent psychotherapy.

I will suggest how current knowledge of babies could affect the feminine, mindful of cultural variations in parenting and open to the criticism of being too simplistic. Leaving aside theoretical debates about connections between femininity and motherhood, I hope to show in two vignettes of brief applications of psychoanalytic theory and mainly short-term clinical interventions in a consultation-liaison service in a busy maternity hospital with 8000 births a year what can be achieved in psychoanalytically derived intervention in the perinatal period. The vignettes describe women with a background of trauma and substance use; I intervened with bonding issues primarily using containment, which can be widely accepted. I will discuss an infant contributing to the feminine, viewing motherhood as a developmental process, and then review the concepts of early Oedipus, and bisexuality, as, without denying the role of envy and aggression from an infant mental health perspective, these concepts may no longer hold centre stage as much as they once did.

Finally, I touch on possible implications for psychoanalytic intervention. It may seem that I am not talking about the feminine but bear with me until the clinical section.

The feminine desire for a baby

Clinical infant mental health perspectives support psychoanalytic views about the desire to care empathically for a baby, to desire and be desired bidirectionally, along with greater freedom to choose not to be a mother. The many assisted reproductive techniques highlight the insistent desire in so many women for a baby: sperm from Australia may be flown to South Africa for a surrogate pregnancy and the resulting baby then flown to Italy for a single 60-year-old father to raise. A baby is often ready at birth to enter a relationship with the joyful feminine, who despite previous ambivalence and anxiety can fall in love with a passion she felt beyond belief the moment she holds her baby and within a month can see the baby’s intense looking at her.

responseSet.title: The growth of mind: by Neville Symington, London and New York, Routledge, 2019, 118pp. £22.49, ISBN: 978 -1-138 327832 (pb), 978-1-138-32781-8 (hb), 978-0-429-44907-9 (eb)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The growth of mind

by Neville Symington, London and New York, Routledge, 2019, 118pp. £22.49, ISBN: 978 -1-138 327832 (pb), 978-1-138-32781-8 (hb), 978-0-429-44907-9 (eb)

Roger Kennedy

Roger Kennedy

London

Roger Kennedy   krogerkennedy@aol.com

It is not an easy task to summarize this book, which, in nine lectures and an introduction, covers a series of complex thoughts, bringing together a lifetime of thinking about the practice of psychoanalysis and the nature of the mind. Neville Symington writes clearly and in a way that engages with the reader, taking them gradually into the depth of his thinking. One feels by the end that there has been a worthwhile journey, opening up areas of thought often not considered, at least in print, by psychoanalytic authors.

Symington summarizes the ambitious goal of his book as coming to an understanding, “of the mind in general and its capacity to discover the truth and to establish this as the foundation stone for our judgement and critique of the human world” (1). His field of exploration lies in the psychological processes encountered in the consulting room. He makes the point early on that his mode of enquiry is different from that generally taught in psychoanalytic institutes, including where he was trained in London. Instead of an approach to psychoanalytic treatment based upon a school of some kind, with various techniques, doctrines, and theories of interpretation with the hope of some “scientific” respectability, he proposes a more open-ended approach responsive to each individual’s unique subjectivity, or what he describes as a person’s “creative core.”

Thus,

Central to this book is the statement that the core of the mind is a creator but just as in the external world there are different products issuing from the creator like paintings, sculpture, and poetry and music so also this creator is able to fashion mental realities that are detached from the senses, or at least from the tactile senses. (17)

This creative core for Symington remains essentially unknowable, though we can know its productions. Thus, the creative core produces assessable dreams and reverie but we cannot assess the creative core itself. For him, this is the unconscious, the creative core of the personality (113). There are obvious overlaps with Freud’s notion of the dream navel as the spot where the dream reaches down into the unknown (Freud 1900, 111n), Winnicott’s notion of the true self from which arises the spontaneous gesture and the personal idea, or philosophical and religious thinking about the nature of the human soul as something irreducible about each person. Symington does make some comparisons with philosophical and religious thinkers, but given the lecture format his treatment of them is relatively brief.

Dream house: an intimate portrait of the Philip Johnson Glass House

by Adele Tutter, Charlottesville, Virginia, University of Virginia Press, 2016, 312 pp., $39.90 (hardcover), ISBN: 978-0813938271

Vera Camden

Vera Camden

Vera Camden   vcamden@kent.edu

Philip Johnson's Glass House has been heralded as “the pinnacle of modernism,” and the crown of the Cleveland-born architect's long and influential career. The Glass House itself is an iconic structure that Johnson occupied as a summer abode. The entire Glass House estate and enterprise comprises 16 buildings, sculptures, structures and objects situated on 40 acres of land in New Canaan, Connecticut that Johnson constructed over some 50 years of his career. The estate, then, is also known as the Glass House, and has been called a “compact critical catalog” of his life's work. Johnson designed numerous famous buildings in cities across America, but it is the Glass House that remains his undisputed masterpiece (Tutter, 2).

Adele Tutter interprets this architectural masterpiece of the twentieth-century International style equipped with the methodology of classical dream interpretation, drawn predominantly from Freud's own masterpiece The Interpretation of Dreams (1900). She views the objects she beholds as pieces of a dream narrative whose manifest and latent content can be discerned through the lineaments of the architect's life and career. Whether looking at the Glass House itself and its contents, or the adjacent, complementary Brick House in which Johnson slept, or the several other constructions established on the estate over time, Tutter considers each and all according to dream interpretation. Johnson's writings, interviews and other materials come to bear a rough equivalence to what the analyst might use as associations to the dream material of their analysand. She draws from the vast web of personal and professional documents concerning Johnson to provide further insight. The Glass House then becomes, among other things, a “landscape of a self” (217) and a materialization of memory from which an intimate portrait of the man and his monuments thus emerges.

616

responseSet.title: Self-insight
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self-insight

Michael Michael

Michael Michael

Seoul
South Korea
Underwood International College

Michael Michael   CONTACT Michael T. Michael mmichael.esq@gmail.com Underwood International College, Yonsei University, 50 Yonsei-ro, Seodaemun-gu, Seoul, 03722 Republic of Korea
Insight, self-knowledge, construal, belief, motion, resistance

Insight is an important notion in psychoanalysis, as it is regarded as the main mediator of psychic change in therapy. In this article I provide an account of a specific kind of insight, which I call self-insight. Self-insight is that which lies at the roots of what Bell and Leite (Bell, D., and A. Leite. 2016. “Experiential self-understanding.” The International Journal of Psychoanalysis 97 (2): 305–332) describe as experiential self-understanding, a process of increasing articulate awareness of one’s psychic life. So conceived, self-insight has four key characteristics: (1) it is distinct from merely intellectual self-knowledge, (2) it arises directly out of first-person experience, (3) it encompasses a lived perspective, and (4) it often requires the overcoming of resistance. My account of self-insight makes use of the notion of construal, a mental state that is constitutive of emotion and plays an important role in motivation. Specifically, I propose that one gains self-insight when one becomes insightfully conscious of a previously unconscious construal, which involves construing one’s construal as the construal it is. This account of self-insight shows how it exhibits the key characteristics described above.

La notion d'insight joue un rôle important en psychanalyse, étant donné que l'insight est considéré comme le principal moteur du changement psychique au cours d'une thérapie. L'auteur de cet article rend compte d'une forme d'insight particulière, qu'il appelle l'insight de soi (self-insight). L'insight de soi est à la racine de ce que Bell et Leite (2016) ont décrit comme une expérience d'auto-compréhension, un processus où qui voit s'accroître chez l'individu la connaissance de sa vie psychique. Vu sous un tel angle, l'insight de soi possède quatre principales caractéristiques : (1) il est à distinguer d'une connaissance de soi purement intellectuelle, (2) il émane directement de l'expérience du sujet, (3) il englobe un point de vue basé sur l'expérience, et (4) il implique le plus souvent de venir à bout d'une résistance. L'auteur utilise la notion de construction (construal), un état psychique qui est constitutif de l'émotion et qui joue un rôle important dans la motivation. Il considère notamment que l'individu accède à cet insight de soi, lorsqu'il prend conscience via un insight d'une construction auparavant inconsciente, ce qui suppose qu'il soit à même d'analyser cette construction pour ce qu'elle est. Cette description de l'insight de soi rend compte de la façon dont sont mises en œuvre les principales caractéristiques évoquées précédemment.

Einsicht spielt in der Psychoanalyse eine wichtige Rolle, weil sie als entscheidender Vermittler von psychischer Veränderung in der Therapie gilt. In diesem Beitrag erläutere ich eine besondere Art der Einsicht, die ich als “Selbsteinsicht” bezeichne. Selbsteinsicht bildet die Grundlage der von Bell und Leite (2016) beschriebenen erfahrungsgestützten Selbstverständigung, eines Prozesses wachsenden artikulierten Gewahrseins des eigenen Seelenlebens. So verstanden, ist die Selbsteinsicht durch vier Schlüsselmerkmale charakterisiert: (1) Sie unterscheidet sich von einer rein intellektuellen Selbsterkenntnis, (2) erwächst unmittelbar aus der eigenen Erfahrung, (3) enthält eine gelebte Perspektive und (4) setzt häufig das Überwinden von Widerständen voraus. In meiner Erläuterung der Selbsteinsicht stütze ich mich auf das Konzept der Interpretation/Auslegung [construal], eines psychischen Zustandes, der für Emotionen konstitutiv ist und für die Motivation eine wesentliche Rolle spielt. Ich vertrete insbesondere die Ansicht, dass man zur Selbsteinsicht gelangt, wenn man sich einer zuvor unbewussten Interpretation durch Einsicht bewusst wird, d.h. sich bewusst machen kann, dass man die Interpretation als die Interpretation, die sie ist, selbst konstruiert. Diese Darlegung der Selbsteinsicht illustriert die oben beschriebenen charakteristischen Merkmale.

Il concetto di insight è importante in psicoanalisi in quanto di solito si vede in esso il principale agente mediatore del cambiamento psichico nella terapia. Nel presente lavoro descriverò un tipo particolare di insight, a cui ho dato il nome di self-insight. Il self-insight sta alla base di quella che Bell e Leite (2016) chiamano “comprensione di sé” esperienziale, un processo mediante cui la consapevolezza della propria vita mentale aumenta e diventa più articolata. Inteso in questo modo, il self-insight ha quattro caratteristiche principali: (1) è diverso dalla conoscenza di sé puramente intellettuale, (2) emerge direttamente da un’esperienza fatta in prima persona, (3) prevede una prospettiva vissuta, e (4) spesso richiede che vengano superate delle resistenze. La mia descrizione del self-insight fa uso del concetto di costruzione, uno stato mentale costitutivo delle emozioni che svolge anche un ruolo importante nella motivazione. Nello specifico, la mia ipotesi è che si acquisisca self-insight nel momento in cui, tramite un insight, si diventa consapevoli di una costruzione rimasta fino a quel momento inconscia; è questo un processo che vede la persona interessata costruire la sua costruzione nei termini della costruzione che essa è. Questo modo di presentare il self-insight mostra come esso possieda le quattro caratteristiche essenziali sopra menzionate.

El concepto de insight es importante en el psicoanálisis, y es considerado el principal mediador del cambio psíquico en la terapia. En este artículo, el autor da cuenta de un tipo específico de insight, al que llama self-insight (insight sobre el self). El self-insight yace en la base de lo que Bell y Leite (2016) describen como la comprensión experiencial del self, un proceso de creciente articulación de la consciencia de la vida psíquica de uno mismo. Así concebido, el self-insight tiene cuatro características principales: (1) es distinto del mero conocimiento intelectual sobre el self, (2) emerge directamente de la experiencia de primera persona (3) engloba una perspectiva vivenciada, y (4) a menudo requiere superar la resistencia. Este self-insight recurre a la noción de construal, un estado mental constitutivo de la emoción, que desempeña un importante rol en la motivación. De modo específico, se propone que uno alcanza el self-insight al llegar a una consciencia con profundo insight de un construal inconsciente previo, lo que implica construir el construal de uno mismo como el construal que es. Esta presentación del self-insight demuestra cómo este manifiesta las características centrales antes descritas.

responseSet.title: Climate change panel report
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Climate change panel report

Lynne Zeavin

Lynne Zeavin

New York City

Lynne Zeavin   drlynne.zeavin@gmail.com

drlynne.zeavin@gmail.com

Chair: Donald Moss

Presenters: Dela Habibi-Kohlen, Luc Magnenat, and Sally Weintrobe

This singularly important panel addressing the environmental crisis was convened on the Saturday morning of the meetings. Donald Moss from New York City opened the panel with a most moving story of a teacher telling the Noah's Ark tale to her class. The children mostly listened attentively, except for one child, Joel, who screamed out, Where are all the bodies? “WHERE ARE ALL THE BODIES OF THE PEOPLE AND THE ANIMALS THAT DIED IN THE FLOOD?” Moss concluded that this child was asking a psychoanalytic question. “No dead bodies appear in his graphic version of the story of Noah, the indelibly standard theological/pedagogical representation of our – yes, our – founding moment. The narrative of this punitive cleansing by water yields only survivors – Noah, his wife, sons and their wives – and, of course, the earth's entire fauna, all coupled, erotically charged, each pair ready to procreate, to go forth and multiply, and refill the Earth with its original animal abundance. Following the child's question, where are all the bodies?, we are then required to ask: How have they been erased? Why have they been erased? What forces have led to their erasure?

The panel (somewhat uncannily!) addressed these problems – and helped us to think about the forces at work in generating the climate catastrophe – along with various ways of looking at and thinking about our own states of mind, and the demand on us as individuals, citizens and psychoanalysts, to respond to this crisis.

Dela Habibi-Kohlen from Germany spoke after Donald Moss. She discussed in detail a variety of “stories” related to a series of in-depth interviews she conducted some years ago that sought to answer the question of how people are dealing with climate change. She found those climate sceptics and those who believe that humans have an impact on the health of the environment, but neither seemed motivated toward individual action. Her interviews gave voice to the multitude of ways that people are managing to bear the climate catastrophe, from outright denial, to disavowal or more ordinary ways of putting it out of our minds.

responseSet.title: Response to Dr O. Eshel’s letter
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Dr O. Eshel’s letter

Alfonso Sánchez-Medina

Alfonso Sánchez-Medina

Alfonso Sánchez-Medina   alsanmed@hotmail.com

I am grateful to Dr Eshel for her comments about the paper “Projective Identification and Telepathic Dreams,” and I am driven to explain my thinking about this topic in further detail. She and I share an interest in this enigmatic topic, which is one of the mysterious phenomena that Freud left us as part of his epistemophilic legacy. Perhaps those of us who have addressed it have done so because of a repeated clinical encounter with these mysterious dreams throughout our clinical practice. As an analyst and as a supervisor, I have had several experiences of this type, yet I published only one case in order to be able to expand on its clinical details.

Eshel’s letter brings to mind some of Bion’s (1967) words about what happens when an analyst reads the work of another analyst:

How is the communication between the psycho-analyst reading and the psycho-analyst writing to be made at least as effective as the communication between analyst and analysand? What has to be communicated is real enough; yet every psycho-analyst knows the frustration of trying to make clear, even to another psycho-analyst, an experience which sounds unconvincing as soon as it is formulated. We may have to reconcile ourselves to the idea that such communication is impossible at the present stage of psycho-analysis. Transformations of the psycho-analytical experience into formulations which effect communication between psycho-analyst and reader, remains an activity to be pursued. (122)

Clearly, these difficulties remain, and much more so when it comes to strange topics such as telepathic dreams.

The main point of Eshel’s letter is related to her rejection of the notion that projective identification (PI) might be the central mechanism in these dreams. She says, “This seems to me a reductive explanation,” and later adds, “I believe that telepathic dreams impel us to go further, beyond projective identification, beyond intersubjectivity, into an enigmatic ‘impossible’ extreme of patient-analyst deep interconnectedness, at-one-ment.

responseSet.title: The Group's Vulnerability to Disaster: Basic Assumption and Work Group Mentalities Underlying Trump's 2016 Election
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Group's Vulnerability to Disaster: Basic Assumption and Work Group Mentalities Underlying Trump's 2016 Election

Conrad Chrzanowski

Conrad Chrzanowski

Conrad Chrzanowski   CONTACT Conrad Stephen Chrzanowski conradchrzanowskiphd@gmail.com 352 7th Avenue, Suite 801, New York, NY 10001, USA

Bion, groups, Trump, basic assumptions, part-object, projective identification, introjective identification, regression, dissociation

Donald Trump’s improbable political rise and unexpected capture of the US presidency during the 2016 presidential election is framed within Wilfred Bion’s (1961) theory of groups. Bion’s concepts of basic assumption phenomena, the group as a part-object mother’s body, the function of specialized work groups, and the roles of introjective and projective identification are explored and elaborated upon in the context of the relation between Trump and his supporters and detractors. Basic assumption phenomena were activated in both groups as each defended against psychotic anxieties tied to work group activity and other threatening aspects of group experience. Trump-as-leader emerged through a neglect of projective identification due to both supporters’ and detractors’ regressive and dissociative tendencies as they relate predominantly to pairing group activity.

L'ascension politique improbable de Donald Trump et sa victoire imprévue aux élections présidentielles de 2016 aux États-Unis sont mis en relation avec la théorie des groupes de Bion (1961). Les concepts de Bion – hypothèses de base, le groupe comme objet partiel du corps de la mère, la fonction des groupes de travail spécialisés et le rôle de l'identification introjective et projective sont étudiés et élaborés dans le cadre de la relation entre Trump, ses supporteurs et ses détracteurs. Les phénomènes des hypothèses de base se sont vus activés dans ces deux groupes, chacun se défendant contre les angoisses psychotiques liées à l'activité des groupes de travail et autres aspects menaçants de l'expérience groupale. Le leadership de Trump s'est imposé au travers de l'ignorance envers les processus d'identification projective, en raison des tendances régressives et dissociatives liées au couplage chez ses partisans comme chez ses détracteurs.

Donald Trumps für unwahrscheinlich gehaltener politischer Aufstieg und sein Sieg im US-Präsidentschaftswahlkampf 2016 werden mit Hilfe von Wilfred Bions Gruppentheorie (Bion 1961) erläutert. Bions Konzepte der Grundannahmenphänomene, der Gruppe als Teilobjekt des mütterlichen Körpers, der Funktion spezialisierter Arbeitsgruppen sowie der Rolle von introjektiven und projektiven Identifizierungen werden erforscht und im Kontext der Beziehung zwischen Trump und seinen Anhängern und Gegnern diskutiert. Grundannahmenphänomene wurden in beiden Gruppen aktiviert, um psychotische, mit der Gruppenaktivität verbundene Ängste und weitere bedrohliche Aspekte der Gruppenerfahrung abzuwehren. Als Führer konnte Trump infolge einer Vernachlässigung der projektiven Identifizierung aufsteigen, der die vorwiegend mit der Aktivität der Paarbildungsgruppe zusammenhängenden regressiven und dissoziativen Tendenzen sowohl der Anhänger als auch der Widersacher zugrunde lagen.

L’improbabile ascesa politica di Donald Trump e la sua inaspettata vittoria alle elezioni presidenziali statunitensi del 2016 verranno esaminate in questo lavoro attraverso la lente interpretativa della teoria dei gruppi di Wilfred Bion (1961). È infatti proprio attraverso l’analisi del rapporto fra Trump e i suoi sostenitori e detrattori che l’articolo si propone di esplorare e approfondire concettualizzazioni bioniane tra cui quelle di assunto di base, di gruppo vissuto come corpo materno nella sua dimensione di oggetto parziale, di funzione dei gruppi di lavoro specializzati, e dei ruoli dell’identificazione proiettiva e introiettiva. Si sosterrà ad esempio come sia nel campo dei sostenitori sia in quello dei detrattori di Trump si siano attivati fenomeni legati alla mentalità in assunto di base, nella misura in cui ciascuna delle due fazioni si difendeva da ansie psicotiche legate all’attivita del gruppo di lavoro e da altri aspetti dell’esperienza di gruppo sentiti come minacciosi. Secondo questa prospettiva, la figura di Trump-come-leader sarebbe emersa dal trascurare l’identificazione proiettiva a causa delle tendenze regressive e dissociative di entrambe le fazioni, operanti in misura prevalente secondo l’assunto di base del ‘pairing’.

En este artículo se analiza el improbable ascenso político de Donald Trump y su inesperada captura de la presidencia de los EE.UU. en las elecciones de 2016 en el marco de la teoría de los grupos de Wilfred Bion (1961). Se exploran y elaboran los conceptos bionianos del fenómeno del supuesto básico, el grupo como objeto parcial del cuerpo materno, la función de los grupos de trabajo especializados, y los roles de la identificación proyectiva e introyectiva en el contexto de la relación entre Trump y sus partidarios y detractores. En ambos grupos se activó el fenómeno del supuesto básico, pues cada uno se defendió de ansiedades psicóticas vinculadas a las actividades del grupo de trabajo y otros aspectos amenazadores de la experiencia grupal. La figura de Trump-como-líder emergió por una desatención a la identificación proyectiva, debido a las tendencias regresivas y disociadoras, tanto de los partidarios como de los detractores, al relacionarse predominantemente con la actividad grupal de apareamiento.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The psychoanalyst and the child: From the consultation to psychoanalytic treatment

by Michel Ody, New York, Routledge, 2019, $39.95, 233 pp. ISBN: 978-1-138-38926-7

Gretchen Schmutz

Gretchen Schmutz

Gretchen Schmutz   gschmutz@alumni.uchicago.edu

In this newly translated book, Michel Ody puts forth his vision of the psychoanalytic treatment of children based upon his many years of work as a psychoanalyst and psychiatric consultant at the Alfred Binet Centre in Paris. His book is ambitious in its scope as he outlines his conception of psychoanalytic work with children from the first meeting to the end of treatment. A central idea in his book is his distinction between his use of intermittent psychoanalytic consultations with parents and children from individual child analysis. In consultations, the work is between the analyst, the child, and his parents. In both consultations and analysis, Ody's aim is to allow the unconscious of the child to be elaborated as he sees this as essential to facilitate development. Ody's book is distinctive in the clarity and specificity with which he defines the role and function of his relationship with a child's parents in achieving this goal.

Consultations function as a mean of encouraging a child and his/her parents towards a receptivity and a sensibility about the unconscious and infantile sexuality. Ody uses consultation both as a therapeutic tool and as a gauge for whether an individual child analysis is recommended. In the consultations, Ody is looking for some degree of progression or movement in associativity. Time between consultations could be weeks, months or longer. Thus, the analyst is assessing the feasibility and necessity of analysis by observing the limits of both the child and the parents to engage in the process. Ody is attempting to increase the parent's unconscious resonance with unconscious dimensions of their child. With parents, this associativity involves the parent bringing forth thoughts, associations, and elaborations to what is presented to them about their child, which adds to the analyst's understanding of the child's relation to his parents. The parent's associativity might arrive in various forms such as offering more detailed information about their child, and might include some information about the parent's own history including traumas, circumstances of loss, oedipal themes, cultural factors, and so forth. Whether or not consultations move on to an analysis depends upon the family. An analysis is less likely to be needed when a child is reacting to a specific event such as a loss or the birth of a sibling as opposed to when a more psychopathological psychic structure has already formed. Ody's use of consultations is also a way of assessing the readiness of a child and his parents to allow for an individual child analysis which helps to avoid premature terminations.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel report: IPA Congress London 2019: COWAP panel on perversion of femininity: the objectification of self

Nancy Goodman

Nancy Goodman

Nancy Goodman   nrgoodmanphd@gmail.com

Chair: Paula L. Ellman

Presenter: Danielle Knafo

Discussants: Cristina Saottini, Cecile Bassen, Patricia Alkolombre

Dr Paula Ellman introduced the panel theme and the speaker and discussants. She recognized the centrality of objectification of the female throughout the world and the importance of hearing from the international panel speakers from Argentina, Italy and the USA.

The case of Barbara

Dr Danielle Knafo described the psychoanalytic treatment of a woman who initially presented as a two-dimensional, Barbie doll-like pleaser. The clinical material covered a five-year treatment and showed the way Barbara developed from a perverse version of feminine identity to a woman with feelings and a full identity involving mature relationships and professional competence. Dr Knafo titled her paper “Out of the dollhouse,” capturing the immersion of Barbara in the Barbie metaphor, which included her obsession with beauty and appearances. Barbara’s analysis resulted in the discovery of her own liberated feelings of anger, envy, competitiveness, and sexuality.

The first encounter of client and therapist resembled a theatre piece – a major enactment – setting up the central themes in Barbara's mind that immediately turned up in the pre-transference. Barbara had gone to the bathroom leaving her beautiful, stunning friend in the waiting room. Dr Knafo had the impression that this gorgeous woman was her patient only to discover the “real” Barbara. The tensions about who is the most beautiful ran high throughout much of the treatment, infiltrating the transference and the countertransference.

Barbara was her mother's admirer as she assumed the position of gazing at her while she put on make-up and accepted her inferior place with regard to her and other beautiful women in her life. Father's attention felt good to Barbara but often involved negative remarks about his daughter's body. Consequently, Barbara created a false self, playing with her Barbie dolls and becoming a woman lacking depth and genitals, just like a Barbie. She cultivated the image of herself as a good girl, all the while harbouring fears about her true feelings and a wish to be separate from her mother. Knafo told us: “Mom perceived and treated Barbara as a narcissistic extension of herself. As her mother was her father's doll, Barbara in turn became her mother's doll.” There was some satisfaction in maintaining the Barbie doll fantasy, including the idealization of her virginity.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transsexualism and transgenderism: Unravelling sex and gender, and abstractions of the sexed body**

Jean-Baptiste Marchand , Elise Pelladeau and François Pommier

François Pommier

Caen
France
Laboratoire de Psychologie de Caen Normandie (LPCN) - EA 7452

Poitiers
France
Laboratoire de Recherche en Psychopathologie: nouveaux symptômes et lien social - Clinique de l’Acte et PsychoSexualité, LRPC - CAPS - EA 4050

Nanterre
France
équipe Approche en Psychopathologie Psychanalytique (A2P), Equipe d’Accueil CLIPSYD - EA 4430

François Pommier  

Transsexualism, transgenderism, gender dysphoria, sexuality, sexuation

Although transgenderism is accorded an increasingly important place at the heart of studies concerning problems of gender nonconformity, it remains a phenomenon that is poorly known, and difficult to define, in particular in its relationship with transsexualism. In fact, in spite of an undeniable kinship between them, these two phenomena can be distinguished one from the other, and each represents a way of relating to the subject of the difference between the sexes. To clarify this subject, this article initially presents their emergence, their commonalities and their differences from a historical point of view. Next, both ways of relating to the difference between the sexes are analysed through two clinical case studies, one of transsexualism and one of transgenderism (from extracts of non-directive clinical interviews, as well as data from the Rorschach test and the Thematic Apperception Test [TAT], analysed by the French psychoanalytic method). At the end of this investigation, it is concluded that the distinction between these two phenomena refers to two abstractions of the difference between the sexes, leading to a transformation that can be pictured as a fulfilment driven by this perception.

Bien qu’il lui soit octroyé une place de plus en plus importante au sein des études des problématiques de non-conformité de genre, le transgenderism reste un phénomène mal connu, difficile à définir et à appréhender, notamment dans son rapport au transsexualisme. En effet, malgré une parenté indéniable entre ceux-ci, ces deux phénomènes se distinguent l’un de l’autre, et traduisent chacun d’une modalité de relation du sujet à la différence des sexes. Pour exposer ce propos, dans un premier temps, cet article présentera d’un point de vue historique leurs émergences, leurs liens et leurs différences. Ensuite, les deux modalités de relation à la différence des sexes seront analysées au travers de deux vignettes cliniques de transsexualisme et de transgenderism (à partir d’éléments d’entretiens cliniques non-directifs, ainsi que de données issues de l’analyse psychanalytique du test du rorschach et du TAT). A l’issue de cette investigation, il est conclu que la distinction entre ces deux phénomènes renvoie à deux abstractions de la différence des sexes appelant à une transformation à concevoir comme une réalisation pulsionnelle par la perception. : le transsexualisme visant à assoir la différence des sexes, le transgenderism se situant plutôt dans une quête de genre narcissique indépendamment du sexe.

Obwohl der Transgenderismus in der Erforschung von Problemen der psychosexuellen Nonkonformität einen zunehmend wichtigen Platz erhält, bleibt er ein wenig bekanntes und insbesondere mit Blick auf seine Beziehung zum Transsexualismus schwierig zu definierendes Phänomen. Tatsächlich lassen sich beide Phänomene trotz ihrer unleugbaren Verwandtschaft voneinander unterscheiden; sie repräsentieren jeweils eine Möglichkeit des Zugangs zum Thema der Unterschiede zwischen den Geschlechtern. Um Licht auf dieses Thema zu werfen, werden das Auftauchen, die Gemeinsamkeiten und die Unterschiede der beiden Phänomene hier zunächst unter einem historischen Blickwinkel dargestellt. Anschließend werden beide Möglichkeiten des Zugangs zu den Unterschieden zwischen den Geschlechtern anhand zweier Fallstudien über Transsexualismus bzw. Transgenderismus analysiert (zugrunde gelegt wurden Auszüge aus nicht-direktiven klinischen Interviews sowie Ergebnisse des Rorschach-Tests und des TAT, die mit der französischen psychoanalytischen Methode analysiert wurden). Diese Untersuchung gelangt zu dem Schluss, dass die Unterscheidung zwischen den beiden Phänomenen zwei Arten betrifft, von den Geschlechtsunterschieden zu abstrahieren. Dies führt zu einer Transformation, die man sich als Trieberfüllung durch die Wahrnehmung vorzustellen hat: Der Transsexualismus ist bestrebt, die Geschlechterdifferenz zu setzen, während sich der Transgenderismus eher im Rahmen einer narzisstischen Suche situiert, die vom Geschlecht unabhängig ist.

Sebbene abbia acquisito un posto di crescente rilievo nell’ambito degli studi sulle problematiche inerenti la non-conformità di genere, il transgenderismo rimane ad oggi un fenomeno poco conosciuto, difficile da definire e da comprendere soprattutto in relazione al suo rapporto con il transessualismo. In effetti, malgrado siano innegabilmente apparentati, questi due fenomeni sono distinti l’uno dall’altro nella misura in cui ciascuno esprime un diverso modo di relazionarsi del soggetto alla differenza tra i due sessi. Allo scopo di portare chiarezza su questo argomento, l’articolo adotterà innanzitutto una prospettiva storica per presentare l’emergere dei due fenomeni, i loro legami e le loro differenze . Verranno poi analizzate le due modalità di relazione rispetto alla differenza sessuale facendo ricorso a due vignette cliniche: una su un caso di transessualismo, e l’altra su un paziente transgender. (In entrambi i casi il materiale è tratto da interviste cliniche non strutturate, oltre che da dati del test di Rorschach e del TAT esaminati seguendo una metodologia psicoanalitica). A conclusione dell’indagine si afferma che la differenza tra i due fenomeni in oggetto va ricondotta al diverso tipo di astrazione che l’uno e l’altro fanno della differenza sessuale: astrazioni che conducono a una trasformazione che va a sua volta intesa come una realizzazione pulsionale ottenuta attraverso la percezione. Nel caso del transessualismo essa ha il fine di stabilire la differenza tra i sessi, mentre il transgenderismo si situa entro una ricerca narcisistica di genere indipendentemente dal sesso.

Aunque le sea otorgado un lugar cada vez más importante al centro de los estudios de las problemáticas de no conformidad de género, el transgénerismo sigue siendo un fenómeno poco conocido, difícil de definir y de entender, particularmente en su relación al transexualismo. En efecto, a pesar de un parentesco innegable entre ambos, estos dos fenómenos se distinguen uno del otro: cada uno revela una forma de relación del sujeto a la diferencia de sexos. Para explicar este enfoque, este artículo presentará en primer lugar, y desde un punto de vista histórico, sus emergencias, sus vínculos y sus diferencias. Luego, ambas formas de relación a la diferencia de sexos serán analizadas a través de dos viñetas clínicas de transexualismo y de transgénerismo (a partir de los elementos de entrevistas clínicas no dirigidas, y de datos extraídos del análisis psicoanalítico de la prueba del rorschach y del TAT). Al final de esta investigación, se concluye que la distinción entre estos dos fenómenos se traduce en dos abstracciones de la diferencia de los sexos relacionadas con una transformación a concebir como una realización pulsional a través de la percepción: el transexualismo que pretende sentar la diferencia de los sexos, el transgénerismo que se sitúa más bien en una búsqueda narcisista de género independientemente del sexo.

responseSet.title: On Bruce Fink’s “The value of the Lacanian approach to analytic practice”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Bruce Fink’s “The value of the Lacanian approach to analytic practice”

Lionel Bailly

Lionel Bailly

Paris, France
London
UK

Lionel Bailly   CONTACT Lionel Bailly l.bailly@ucl.ac.uk UCL Psychoanalysis Unit, 1-19 Torrington Place, London, WC1E 7HB, UK

Bruce Fink starts his paper by pointing out that there is “no single Lacanian approach to psychoanalytic practice.” This is accurate, firstly, because it is unclear what it is to be Lacanian. When in 1980, having dissolved the institution he had created, Lacan travelled to Caracas to address his South American “pupils,” he stated: “I come here before launching my Freudian Cause. You can see how important this adjective is to me. You’ll be Lacanian if you want. Me; I am Freudian.” (Lacan 1980) In addition, Lacan never defined the canon of an orthodox Lacanian practice as he was discovering it all his life, through experimentations with the Freudian model. On one hand, Lacan practised in different ways when he was a young analyst, a less young analyst and eventually a great master, and with this particular analysand or that one. On the other, he contributed an enormous theoretical edifice, which remained for a long time unwritten. Bruce Fink, humbly but also realistically, suggests that the Lacanian community may shiver at the fact that it is an American analyst who has been granted the prestigious task of writing this paper in the holy journal of the IPA, an organisation that Lacan, once president of the Société Psychanalytique de Paris and training analyst, never wanted to leave. Maybe his own grasp of Lacanian theory is “thought by some in the Lacanian community to be overly direct, transparent, and simplistic … ” but what makes him particularly fit to achieve this challenging task is twofold: there is no doubt that he has read Lacan, and he is an outsider. Having “devoted the past 35 years to grappling with and translating Lacan’s often difficult work,” Fink has not only read a great deal more than most but also had to ponder the meaning of the text. Beyond negotiating the didactic function of its ambiguity, metaphors and jokes, the fruitful digressions about the previous evening’s conference somewhere or a veiled reference to a friend’s clever and useful comment, translating Lacan’s text means settling on a meaning. In addition, a career in American academia placed Fink outside the grip of psychoanalytic institutions and allowed him to say what he wished without too much concern about the impact it could have on his future.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Silent virtues: patience, curiosity, privacy, intimacy, humility, and dignity, by Salman Akhtar

Richard Tuch

Richard Tuch

Los Angeles
M.D. Training and Supervising Analyst

Richard Tuch   rtuch@aol.com

Beyond a doubt, few living psychoanalysts are as prolific as Salman Akhtar. When awarded the prestigious Sigourney Award in 2012, Dr Akhtar already had 300 works in print. Since then he has published another 36 articles and an additional 32 books (a quarter of which he co-edited), leaving us to wonder how on earth he does it. Silent Virtues: Patience, Curiosity, Privacy, Intimacy, Humility, and Dignity is one of six books Akhtar has written or co-edited in 2018 alone. He has generously lent his name to volumes written by collaborators on such topics as arrogance, revenge, regret, jealousy, intimacy, spirituality, and human goodness. Clearly, Dr Akhtar feels he has a lot to say and our field has collectively benefited from his willingness to tirelessly put pen to paper.

In this particular volume, Dr Akhtar continues his tradition of addressing topics many analysts might think about but have yet to seriously consider. Reading this volume leaves one struck by Akhtar's scholarship, evident in his writings about each of the six “virtues” he identifies, which together constitute the subtitle of his book. In each successive chapter, Akhtar presents a 411

Charles Baekeland

Charles Baekeland

Spain

Charles Baekeland   CONTACT Charles Baekeland cebaekeland@hotmail.com Calle Argensola 2 - 44, 28004 Madrid
IPSO, insular tendencies, free thinking, burnout, light-heartedness, depth versus ponderousness

In this article the author attempts to show how participating in an international community of psychoanalysts can have a transformative effect on a psychoanalyst’s training and practice. The article focuses on the beneficial consequences for candidates of being involved in the International Psychoanalytical Studies Organization (IPSO), due to certain felicitous specificities of this organization, though much of what is discussed can be applied to being involved in other organizations. At the level of training, insular tendencies and local institutional dynamics can limit candidates to a restricted range of thinking, combined with emotional entanglements, which may curtail their intellectual freedom. The breadth of differences in approaches to training that IPSO exposes candidates to, and the fact that this takes place outside of their home institute and its hierarchies, is potentially an antidote to this. At the level of practice, psychoanalysis is a rewarding but also arduous profession that exposes its practitioners to many hardships, the extent of which is sometimes minimized or denied. IPSO embodies a spirit that marries intense intellectual interest for psychoanalysis with an enthusiastic attitude towards sharing good times with colleagues. The author posits that this libidinal investment is paramount to surviving and enjoying our work.

L'auteur de cet article cherche à montrer comment la participation à une communauté internationale de psychanalystes peut exercer un effet de transformation sur la formation et la pratique des psychanalystes. L'auteur souligne les bénéfices que les candidats peuvent tirer de leur implication dans l'Organisation des études psychanalytiques (IPSO), en raison des avantages spécifiques offerts par cet organisme, bien que cela puisse également s'appliquer tout aussi bien à l'implication des candidats dans d'autres organismes. Au niveau de la formation, les tendances insulaires et la dynamique institutionnelle locale peuvent limiter l'espace de pensée des candidats, sans parler des interférences émotionnelles qui contribuent elles aussi à brider leur liberté intellectuelle. L'éventail des différences relatives aux modèles de formation auquel l'IPSO expose les candidats - le fait aussi que tout ceci se produise à l'extérieur de leur institut de formation et sa hiérarchie - peut jouer le rôle d'un antidote. Au niveau de la pratique, la psychanalyse est une profession aussi enrichissante qu'ardue qui expose ses praticiens à bien des épreuves dont l'étendue est parfois minimisée ou déniée. L'IPSO incarne un état d'esprit qui allie une curiosité intellectuelle intense à un enthousiasme vis à vis du plaisir de la rencontre avec d'autres collègues. L'auteur émet l'hypothèse que cet investissement libidinal est essentiel au fait de survivre à la tâche qui est la nôtre et d'en tirer satisfaction. De plus, dans une profession aussi intangible que la nôtre, les liens personnels et positifs qu'on peut tisser avec des collègues s'avèrent essentiels en ce qu'ils favorisent l'efficacité de la communication de nos façons de penser.

In diesem Beitrag versucht der Autor zu zeigen, dass die Teilhabe an einer internationalen Gemeinschaft von Psychoanalytikern die Ausbildung und Praxis des Einzelnen beeinflussen und verändern kann. Das Augenmerk richtet sich auf die Vorteile und Besonderheiten, von denen Kandidaten profitieren, wenn sie sich in der International Psychoanalytical Studies Organization (IPSO) engagieren, die aber zu einem Großteil auch für das Engagement in anderen, entsprechenden Organisationen gelten. Auf der Ausbildungsebene können Abschottungstendenzen und die Dynamik der örtlichen Institute den Denkhorizont der Kandidaten einengen. Hinzu kommen emotionale Verwicklungen, die sie in ihrer intellektuellen Freiheit beschränken. Die große Bandbreite an unterschiedlichen Ausbildungsgängen von IPSO-Kandidaten, die man außerhalb der Heimatinstitute und ihrer Hierarchien kennenlernen kann, wirkt hier als ein gesundes Gegenmittel. Auf der Praxisebene ist die Psychoanalyse eine lohnende, aber auch fordernde Profession, die für den Ausübenden zahlreiche Mühen mit sich bringt, deren Ausmaß bisweilen bagatellisiert oder verleugnet wird. IPSO verkörpert einen Geist, der das intensive intellektuelle Interesse an der Psychoanalyse mit einer Begeisterung für kollegiale Geselligkeit teilt. Der Autor vertritt die Ansicht, dass diese libidinöse Besetzung entscheidend dafür ist, dass wir unsere Arbeit überleben und Freude an ihr haben. Darüber hinaus sind positive persönliche Bindungen in einer Profession wie der unseren für den fruchtbaren Gedankenaustausch unverzichtbar.

In questo articolo l’autore prova a mostrare come la partecipazione a una comunità internazionale di psicoanalisti possa avere un effetto trasformativo sul training e sulla pratica analitica. Particolare attenzione viene dedicata ai benefici che il coinvolgimento nelle attività dell’Organizzazione Internazionale per gli Studi Psicoanalitici (IPSO) può portare ai candidati analisti. Per quanto tali benefici siano in parte dovuti a caratteristiche specifiche di questa organizzazione, molte delle pratiche qui discusse si possono applicare anche alla partecipazione ad altre organizzazioni. Per ciò che riguarda il training, la tendenza all’autoisolamento e le dinamiche locali delle istituzioni possono limitare i candidati restringendone l’orizzonte di pensiero; a ciò si aggiunge il fatto che gli intricati nodi affettivi spesso presenti in questi contesti possono comprometterne la libertà intellettuale. La grande varietà di approcci al training a cui la IPSO espone i candidati e il fatto stesso che, frequentandola, questi ultimi si trovino fuori dal loro istituto di riferimento e dall’influsso delle sue gerarchie, rappresenta già di per sé un potenziale antidoto contro questi aspetti così limitanti. A livello di pratica occorre riconoscere che, pur dando grandi soddisfazioni, la psicoanalisi è anche una professione assai ardua ed espone chi la pratica a non poche difficoltà, la cui portata viene talora minimizzata quando non addirittura negata. La IPSO incarna uno spirito che coniuga un forte interesse intellettuale per la psicoanalisi a una spinta entusiastica verso la condivisione di esperienze positive con i colleghi. L’autore sostiene che questo investimento libidico è fondamentale non solo per sopravvivere al nostro lavoro, ma altresì per poterne godere. Inoltre, in una professione per tanti versi poco definibile come la nostra, i legami personali positivi con i colleghi aumentano in misura molto significativa l’efficacia con cui possiamo comunicare il nostro pensiero.

El autor intenta mostrar cómo la participación en una comunidad internacional de psicoanalistas puede tener un efecto transformador en la formación y en la práctica de los psicoanalistas. El artículo se centra en las consecuencias beneficiosas para los candidatos de ser parte de la Organización Internacional de Estudios Psicoanalíticos (IPSO, por sus siglas en inglés) debido a acertadas especificidades de esta organización, aunque gran parte de lo discutido puede aplicarse a la participación en otras organizaciones. En la esfera de la formación, las tendencias insulares y las dinámicas institucionales locales pueden restringir a los candidatos a una gama limitada de ideas, junto con atrapamientos emocionales, que pueden reducir su libertad intelectual. Un potencial antídoto a esto es la amplitud de diferentes enfoques a la formación que expone IPSO a los candidatos, y el hecho de que esta exposición se lleva a cabo fuera de los institutos de origen y sus jerarquías. En la esfera de la práctica, el psicoanálisis es gratificante pero también una profesión ardua que expone a sus profesionales a muchas dificultades, cuya magnitud es a veces minimizada y negada. IPSO encarna un espíritu que conjuga el interés intelectual intenso por el psicoanálisis con la actitud entusiasta hacia el compartir buenos momentos con colegas. El autor plantea que esta inversión libidinal es crucial para sobrevivir y disfrutar el trabajo psicoanalítico. Además, en una profesión tan intangible como la de los psicoanalistas, los lazos personales positivos con colegas son esenciales para la comunicación efectiva de ideas.

responseSet.title: On dangerous ground: Freud’s visual cultures of the unconscious: by Diane O’Donoghue, New York, Bloomsbury Academic, 2019, $118.44, 400pp. ISBN: 978-1501327957.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On dangerous ground: Freud’s visual cultures of the unconscious

by Diane O’Donoghue, New York, Bloomsbury Academic, 2019, $118.44, 400pp. ISBN: 978-1501327957.

Anne Cheng

Anne Cheng

USA

Anne Cheng   aacheng@Princeton.edu

Objects are curiously absent in Freudian psychoanalysis. Although Freud clearly had a penchant for thinking with and through things, especially aesthetic things—paintings, artefacts, antiquities, dolls, pearl drop earrings, reticules, etc.—the materiality of objectness did not seem to 798 interest him very much beyond its service as a vehicle for other signification. For Freud, things disappear into metaphors; matter dissolves into symptoms. The “object” of psychoanalysis more often than not denotes not real things or even persons but a structural position in a psychical grammar, that is, object as one side of the subject/object dyad and understood as “the thing in regard to which and through which the instinct is able to achieve its aim.” (Freud, Instincts and Their Vicissitudes, SE XIV, 122). In short, objects are always what the poet T. S. Eliot calls “objective correlatives” for Freud. Indeed, this move away from material reality to psychical reality is a hallmark of the origin of psychoanalysis. Freud, as the story goes, abandoned his conviction of the real roots of pathogenic infantile scenes in favour of psychical reality and, in doing so, uncovered universal pathology and psychical reality itself.

Diane O’Donoghue's new study On Dangerous Ground: Freud's Visual Cultures of the Unconscious cuts against this grain and reasserts material culture firmly back into the making of psychoanalysis, arguing that Freud's preoccupation with infantile psychical erotic life is itself a screen for the trauma of his own very material immigration experience and the very real social, economic, familial, and affective displacements that it engendered. Through painstaking research, original archival findings, and her own retracing of Freud's journey from his childhood home of Moravia to his new life in Vienna and its physical circumstances, O’Donoghue uncovers a phase of Freud's own self-proclaimed “unremarkable” and “forgettable” childhood in order to show how “Freud may have selected the moment of sexual ‘awakening’ to create an overly determined erotic scenario that could serve to subsume other experiences” (127). Revisiting Freud's account of the origin of his discovery of infantile desire on a train ride relocating him from Moravia to Vienna, O’Donoghue narrates instead a compelling story of migration, of transition and transit that Freud's insistence on the internal vicissitudes of childhood fantasies and sexual desires obfuscates. In short, O’Donoghue demonstrates that Freud's discovery of repression was itself a repression. Or, to put it differently, Freud found forgetfulness in order to forget.

responseSet.title: Panel on: “Transformations in female bodily experiences and bodily metaphors”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel on: “Transformations in female bodily experiences and bodily metaphors”

Margaret Fitzpatrick Hanly

Margaret Fitzpatrick Hanly

Margaret Fitzpatrick Hanly   mafhanly@gmail.com

Chair: Margaret Ann Fitzpatrick Hanly

Presenters: Roberto Muñoz, Marina Altmann, Beatriz de Leon Bernardi, Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber

The presentations on this panel brought to life the “transformations in the female bodily experiences and bodily metaphors” in a seven-year psychoanalysis, with detailed session verbatim transcriptions by the analyst. The presentations also portrayed how this systematic approach to clinical verbatim transcriptions (the Three-Level Model [3-LM]), in this panel focusing on female bodily metaphors, can bring psychoanalysts from different cultures together to formulate transformations in the patient, with remarkable consensus.

Roberto Muñoz (Madrid Psychoanalytic Association) opened his presentation with Freud's “ideal ambition” for psychoanalysis to become a scientific discipline within the natural sciences, adding that this remains “one of the most controversial and debated issues, both inside and outside psychoanalytic institutions”. Munoz raised questions concerning convergences in theories, which may provide a “common ground”, and scientific pluralism with the problem of “many psychoanalyses”. He described the 3-LM as a guide for clinical discussions, which respond to these important questions. He presented observations on change in a patient presenting to a 3-LM group in Madrid.

Munoz concluded his presentation saying that he strongly supported the systematic work of clinical observation groups, which responds to urgent questions for contemporary psychoanalysis and provides a useful method for improving the training of psychoanalysts. Munoz stated: “since clinical experiences and evidence, based on data from psychoanalytic research, have confirmed that psychoanalytic treatments produce significant changes in patients, our current mission needs to include: ‘how to describe more precisely what these observed changes are’ and, finally, ‘to clarify how and why patients change’”.

Marina Altmann (Uruguayan Psychoanalytic Association) presented the transformations seen in Ms C, a single 35-year-old professional of mixed race, who was living a “polyamorous” lifestyle when she began analysis. She reported risky behaviours involving drugs and casual sex with heterosexual couples, with men “on the corner” and with women. Ms C dressed in a “sexualized and provocative style”, provoking feelings in the analyst that varied from “very protective, to disgusted”. The analyst experienced intense anxiety in the countertransference in early years of the analysis.

responseSet.title: Deconstructing the feminine: Discourses, logics and power. Theoretico-clinical implications
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Deconstructing the feminine: Discourses, logics and power. Theoretico-clinical implications

Leticia Glocer Fiorini

Leticia Glocer Fiorini

Full member and former President of the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association

Leticia Glocer Fiorini   leticiaglocer@gmail.com Zapiola 1646, CABA 1426, Buenos Aires, Argentina

This article begins with a discussion of the polysemy of the category of “feminine,” proposing a distinction between the feminine, femininity, female sexuality, maternity and women, while also establishing their interrelatedness. She approaches “the feminine” as a symptom of culture. The article examines: (a) the complex interconnections between psychoanalysis and each era’s prevailing discourses on sexual difference, the feminine and women, (b) the power relations between genders, (c) the logics used to think about these problems in psychoanalytic theory, and their effects upon clinical psychoanalysis. The following questions are considered: individual and collective changes in the feminine position over recent decades and the transformations of traditional roles, masculine and feminine. Against the backdrop of the growth in new reproductive techniques, non-conventional familial configurations, the expansion of cyberculture and globalization phenomena, they are examined. These factors prompt a rethinking of the construction of subjectivity from the perspective of the manifold itineraries of desire, which always functions in excess vis-à-vis the norm, as well as the plurality of identifications at play. The article highlights the difficulties in assigning women the position of subject, a hangover from modernity. This is evidenced by the limits of many theories in addressing this problem. The article incorporates clinical illustrations, pointing out that a single clinical reference does not do justice to the variety of problems and dilemmas that may be observed in the field of the feminine. The concept of viewpoint is considered in relation to the construction of theories of the feminine and women, with the hypothesis that there is no presumed neutrality of thought or language, rather a subject, in the masculine position, that constructs the theory regarding sexual difference and the masculine-feminine pair. Castration anxiety and societal norms and discourse mutually reinforce one another in this construction. In response to the theoretico-clinical impasses and blind spots in relation to the feminine which may be observed in psychoanalytic praxis, the article discusses other logics to think about the feminine. Going beyond the notion of a feminine essence, it suggests a way of thinking about this category that is multi-determinate and web-like, underpinned by post-binary logics, and not confined to one of the poles of a masculine-feminine binary organization. This entails a sustained coexistence of both logics. The article seeks to transcend masculine-feminine dualism while incorporating it into greater complexities. This involves engaging with the opacity of the category of “difference”; re-examining the concept of sexual difference; including other conceptualizations of difference as symbolic operatory and taking a critical approach to the 593

responseSet.title: Everyday evils: a psychoanalytic view of evil and morality by C. Covington
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Everyday evils: a psychoanalytic view of evil and morality by C. Covington

Danae Karydaki

Danae Karydaki

Papagou
Athens
Greece

Danae Karydaki   dkarydaki@psych.uoa.gr

The notion of evil has troubled humans for as long as human activity has been recorded. Historical sources reveal that religion was the first cultural product that proposed a conceptual tool to approach evil; asa rule, people in ancient paganistic cultures tried to explain evil as an act of gods, whereas, in Abrahamic religions, evil was understood as a human choice associated with disobedience to the single God. Philosophy, a systematic method to approach knowledge that emerged in the 6th-century BCE Greek world, devoted one of its fundamental branches, Ethics, to the study of good and evil; some of the world’s greatest philosophers, such as Aristotle, Thomas Aquinas, Baruch Spinoza, Immanuel Kant and, most recently, Hannah Arendt, worked extensively on these issues. In the early twentieth century, Sigmund Freud pioneered a new theoretical model as well as clinical practice that could, among other things, be employed for a more integrated understanding of evil: psychoanalysis. In her book Everyday Evils: A Psychoanalytic View of Evil and Morality (2017), the psychoanalyst Coline Covington investigates this ever-intriguing but, apparently, under-researched question of evil through a psychoanalytic prism.

The book consists of nine small chapters, an introduction, and a conclusion. “Evil” according to Covington’s apt and articulate definition

is an extreme form of destructiveness that dehumanizes people, reducing them to objects without minds or feelings, to be used by others as means to an end and disregarded as ends in themselves. Evil also entails choice. It is different from an act of nature that arbitrarily wipes out individuals and groups of people. It is an act that targets its victims or class of victims and requires the force of decision [4].

In the book chapters, written in the course of three years, the author attempts to answer various questions regarding evil in the order that they “arose in [her] mind over this period of time” [2]. In chapter 1, she begins her exploration by tracing the links between evil and a concept all too familiar to psychoanalysts: human destructiveness. As perhaps anticipated, she locates the roots of the former in the latter, while employing Donald Winnicott’s theory of infant aggression to map the origins of evil. According to Covington, in our effort to understand evil as a perversion, Winnicott’s theory of aggression is more convincing than Freud’s or Klein’s because it emphasises the ways in which the response of the object shapes and mediates the individual’s own experience of their destructiveness during the early stages of an infant’s life.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

British psychoanalysis: New perspectives in the Independent tradition

edited by Gregorio Kohon, New Library of Psychoanalysis, London, Routledge, 2018, £29.99, ISBN 9781138579057

Ch. Atlan

Ch. Atlan

Ch. Atlan   lechartier-atlan@wanadoo.fr

This fascinating book is required reading for anyone who wants to understand the polyphonic texture of British psychoanalysis. Kohon himself is a good illustration—born in Argentina, he trained in England, left to live for a time in Australia, where he founded and ran a centre for psychoanalytic studies with his wife and returned to England, where he became a training analyst and co-authored a book with André Green; this already testifies to a vast international culture. He shows an admirable breadth of reading and capacity for synthesis. His book is entitled British Psychoanalysis: New Perspectives in the Independent Tradition, indicating that it is a sequel to The British School of Psychoanalysis: The Independent Tradition, published in 1986. It is these new perspectives that I will address, while heeding his statement that this is not just a new edition but a new book.

Kohon asserts the subjective quality of his book and his choice of authors. He has kept the four major themes of 1986: the early environment, the analytic encounter, regression and female sexuality. All these express strong elements of Independent thought. He takes as reference points a large proportion of the texts of the period—tradition implies being rooted in history—but he has the excellent idea of having these commented on by younger Independent authors such as Josh Cohen, Steven Groarke, Hannah Browne and Megan Virtue.

What is this Independent tradition? The first part of the book is mainly devoted to this reflection, which combines the history of ideas and history. Kohon shares the task with Rosine Perelberg (2008), to whom the title of his article, “Looking Back into the Future” pays implicit tribute in its consideration of time and deferred action or Nachträglichkeit, an important element I think of the “new perspectives.”

There is no uniformity in Independent thought, which incorporates diversity. Perelberg, a corresponding member of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society, refers to a “multi-dimensional frame of reference.” More than an organisation or a group in the strict sense, this concerns a state of mind that leaves room for the desire to “speak one's own language” to quote Winnicott (1987), a major figure among the Independents, and excludes any theoretical dogmatism. What the Independents have in common is their rejection of submission to either of the two existing groups, the Anna Freudians (or “B” group) and the Kleinians. The Independents are in the middle, as “the Middle Group” (Glover), “non-aligned,” or “muddled group” according to their adversaries.

[[RunningHead]]

PANEL Report, 51st IPA Congress London 2019: The male analyst as the feminine object

Sylvia Zwettler-Otte

Sylvia Zwettler-Otte

Sylvia Zwettler-Otte   Sylvia.zwettler-otte@wpv.at

Chair: Cláudio Eizirik

Panellists: Michael J. Diamond, Stefano Bolognini, Fred Busch

Reporter: Sylvia Zwettler-Otte

In his introduction Dr Cláudio Eizirik, Training Analyst of the Porto Alegre Psychoanalytic Society and IPA President 2005–2009, pointed at Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel's paper from 1988: she described the femininity of the analyst's work and the balanced mixture of feminine and masculine traits, resulting from our own maternal and paternal identifications. She considered the analytic setting as a representation of the mother's lap, but also as the law that represents the father.

The first presenter, Dr Michael J. Diamond, Full Member of the Los Angeles Institute and Society for Psychoanalytic Studies, started his paper “The Elusiveness of the Feminine” with a joke: according to a “less androcentric version,” God created Eve first but had to suggest to her that she let Adam believe that he was first, because he needed it. This “cognizance of the ‘other’ might more naturally reflect” our thoughts about the “feminine.” Diamond considered the psychoanalytic and cultural changes during the last decades and mentioned Freud's limited view due to his perspective of masculine subjectivity, but he also stressed the fact that Freud nevertheless helped us see that psychosexuality rests upon unconscious fantasies.

Diamond was also aware of the unavoidable limitations and influences here and now in this panel, the risks of conflating the female and the maternal, the problem of disentangling femininity and masculinity from biological sex, etc.: “we are speculating on the topic – a panel of four heterosexual, aging, white male analysts from different regions of the Westernized world, each shaped by our idiosyncratic psychodynamics and psychocultural environments.”

Diamond referred to the facets of contemporary analytic thinking (Balsam 2001, 2019; Fiorini 2007, 2017; Irigaray 1993; Schiller 2018), which tends not to focus in the constructed feminine on the experience of lack and to locate thrusting power as male and receptive cavities as female, but tries to integrate unconscious fantasies of invasion and unrelenting bodily pain, of the birthing mother's body containing an “other within the self.” Diamond hoped that panels like this one might help to include penetrative and generative forms of potency as well as an active receptivity and permeability and to recognize the mutative role played by the analyst’s gender fluidity. He suggested four tropes for imagining the feminine in the male (and the female) analyst:

receptivity
