responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel on “A clinician’s view of research. Critical thinking in the practice and transmission of psychoanalysis”

Beatriz de León de Bernardi

Beatriz de León de Bernardi

Beatriz de León de Bernardi   deleon.bea@gmail.com

Chair: Rudi Vermote

Presenters: Marina Altmann de Litvan, Beatriz de León de Bernardi, Judy Kantrowitz, Mary Hepworth

Marina Altmann first underlined concepts from different authors – Otto Kernberg, Ricardo Bernardi, Morris Eagle and Linda Goodman – on critical thinking in the transmission of psychoanalysis.

She stated that critical thinking is the analysis of facts to form a judgement. It is a purposeful, self-regulatory judgement with results in interpretation, analysis, evaluation and inference, as well as explanation of the evidential, conceptual, methodological, criteriological or contextual considerations upon which that judgement is based.

Second, Altmann presented results of a research study on psychoanalytic training and psychoanalytic professional practice in Latin America. (2005–2006), in which the researchers investigated how analytical training prepares for the performance of analysts as professionals. Findings showed that the analysts’ self-perception (after five years of being an analyst), despite their not entirely favourable view of the context, was to maintain their main professional identity as psychoanalysts: 77% of the analysts said it was very likely that they would maintain their professional identity as psychoanalysts in five years’ time, and 83% said it was very likely that they would apply their analytical capacity in their clinical practice. There was a strong feeling and desire to continue developing the analytical function: 99% said that their professional identity would be to be an analyst in the next five years and to apply their analytical capacity in their clinical practice. A strong analytical identity was envisioned, but an important number of analysts nevertheless perceived difficulties for the future of psychoanalysis (in some countries more than in others).

In general, there was an opinion that their own training analysis had helped analysts improve their understanding of themselves (4.3 using the following scale: 1, Nothing; 2, Little; 3, More or Less; 4, Quite; 5, Much), the ability to analyse their own behaviour (4.19), feeling in touch with their emotions (4.16) and feeling integrated as a person (4.08). The points on which the analysis were evaluated as least helpful were caring about the others’ opinion of oneself (3.54) and being optimistic about the future (3.58).

responseSet.title: At war with the obvious: disruptive thinking in psychoanalysis by Donald Moss
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

At war with the obvious: disruptive thinking in psychoanalysis by Donald Moss

Hillery Bosworth

Hillery Bosworth

New York
USA
Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research

Hillery Bosworth   hb2082@cumc.columbia.edu

A prolific American analyst, Donald Moss has previously published two collections, Hating in the First Person Plural (2003) and Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Man (2012), which explore the dynamics of misogyny, racism and homophobia and the dilemmas faced by men in search of definition amid ever-shifting notions of masculinity. In At War With the Obvious, Moss continues with these themes under a broader attack on what he sees as kinds of thinking that stagnate the psychoanalytic project, and share, however distantly, dynamic roots with prejudice. Throughout the book, Moss admonishes us to maintain our vigor through constant vigilance against what he alternately calls “the obvious” and “common sense.” By those terms, he refers to received psychoanalytic theory and cultural assumptions applied lazily to clinical work and to interpretations of broader social phenomena. For Moss, discomfort is the signal that we are in the zone where creative work can be done. He reminds us that our vitality comes from ideas that crack existing molds, but that with time these ideas become comfortable and enter the realm of common sense. Common sense thinking, a form of “coasting” on the brave and arduous work of our psychoanalytic forbears, is a defensive mode of pseudo-work aimed at assuaging the clinician’s anxiety about confusional states at the expense of their creative potential. Moss entreats us to join him in his fight against comfortable complacency, so that we may generate new disruptive ideas in our theory and in our individual work, and remain relevant interpreters of the wider world.

In this collection of 11 essays and a chapter of poems, Moss takes his fight to a broad array of topics: work, desire, creativity, masculinity, forgiveness, climate change denial, the concept of perversion and the pitfalls of applied psychoanalysis. His style, inseparable from his thesis, is associative. He warns linearity-seeking readers that “the path to my argument will be indirect” (p. 80). He calls one chapter an “extended meditation” (p. 90). Jumping from metatheory and philosophy – examining the works of Freud, Adorno, Bion, Nietzsche and many others – to portraits of his interior life worthy of gifted novelist, he then swerves abruptly to the realm of art and social phenomena. A single essay, for example, presents five clinical vignettes, biblical scripture, a Kafka story, the Syrian refugee crisis, the AME church killings, and Moss’s day at the gym, each element rigorously examined and connected to the others. As you can imagine, these chapters defy synopsis, but I will describe a few, hoping to capture a glimpse of his arresting voice and provocative ideas in all their richness and complexity.

[[RunningHead]]

Field theory in child and adolescent psychoanalysis: understanding and reacting to unexpected developments, by Elena Molinari

Mary Brady

Mary Brady

San Francisco
California
USA

Mary Brady   mbrady66@earthlink.net

In this, her first book, Elena Molinari places herself on the roster of major psychoanalytic thinkers through her creative theoretical and clinical work, particularly informed by the visual arts. I say psychoanalytic and not solely child psychoanalytic as her book is replete with implications for the analysis of adults drawn from her analyses of children. Child analysis informs adult analysis through its greater emphasis on shapes and forms, which yield the beginnings of representability. While child analysis can sometimes seem like something off to the side or on the edges of psychoanalysis, this book conveys a conviction I share—that child psychoanalysis prefigures Bionian developments in our field (Brady 2018a). Molinari asserts that child analysis is not an application of adult analysis but the catalyst for Bion’s epistemological revolution. Bion’s second analyst, Melanie Klein's major theoretical innovations were based especially on the play of the very young children she analysed. Molinari suggests (and I heartily agree) that: “Bion integrated into his thoughts the insights yielded by child analysis into the origins of thinking and the earliest forms of mental activity” (3). 415

Giorgio Campoli   giorgiocampoli@alice.it

1,2Psicoanalisi delle psicosi is divided into Introduction: by the three editors, and four parts: (1) General Perspectives on the Psychoanalytic Experience with Psychosis: Didier Houzel (France), John Kafka (USA), Franco De Masi (Italy), Riccardo Lombardi (Italy); (2) Communication and Empathy with the Psychotic Analysand: Mark J. Blechner (USA), Michael Robbins (USA), Stefano Calamandrei (Italy), Sarantis Thanopulos (Greece); (3) Particularities of Thought: Giuseppe Civitarese (Italy), Luigi Rinaldi (Italy), Paul Williams (Great Britain); (4) Flexible Integration between Psychoanalysis and Pharmacology: Ira Steinman (USA), Giuseppe Martini (Italy).

From the first lines of text, the editors—Lombardi, Rinaldi, and Thanopulos—are specific about their basic assumptions:

The realm of psychotic experience contains potential knowledge that is still largely unexplored: a potential whose exploration can enrich all persons interested in the human condition, but especially professionals and workers in mental health. We are convinced, in fact, that psychoanalysis can be enormously enriched by the exploration of the psychoses as a basis of renewal. (2)3

The principal road towards this enrichment, in their opinion, lies in “relocating our science in its clinical origin, reactivating the exploration of the more extreme forms in which mental discomfort is presented” (2).

These are statements that, in my opinion, join the scientific undertaking to the ethical one, and that sweep the field clear of the risk of slipping into intellectual and pretentious perspectives. These are the same perspectives held by certain sectors of the anti-psychiatric feeling of 428

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Œdipe médecin (séparation, dépression, sublimation)

[Oedipus as therapist (separation, depression, sublimation)], by Paul Denis, Paris, Presses universitaires de France, 2017, 237 pp., €24. ISBN: 9782130797982

Bernard Brusset

Bernard Brusset

Bernard Brusset   bernard.brusset@wanadoo.fr

The style of this book is enjoyable and straightforward, free from jargon and neologisms; it is nourished by the author’s intimate knowledge of the classics of French literature. The title requires an explanation. The reference to Oedipus is not a reference to the unconscious complex involving incestuous desire and the wish for the death of the rival, the complex at the origin of the neuroses. It designates the therapeutic elaboration of that complex in an analytical setting, in particular as it applies to cases of mourning, trauma and “sexual madness”. The oedipal structuring of desires and identifications that is integral to sexual and generational differentiation and psychical bisexuality is what enables the overcoming and regulation of conflict, as does the institution of a protective superego.

The author’s many original developments are all rooted in his clinical experiences as an analyst, his understanding of the characters of literature and his own theses, which he has defended and exemplified for 20 years (Emprise et satisfaction. Les deux formants de la pulsion [Mastery and Satisfaction. The Two Formative Components of the Drive], Paris PUF, 1998). Since the book reviewed here contains many references to this earlier work, it is necessary to start by outlining its main ideas. Like Jones, Laplanche and many others, the author rejects the notion of the death drive and only retains some aspects of Freud’s second topographical model. He does not replace the death drive with the drive for mastery but proposes a new theoretical model of the drive in which he foregrounds the conjunction and disjunction of two formative components of the drive, both libidinal: a force that cathects satisfaction and another current of cathexis that is in the “mode of mastery”. His starting point is the distinction between desire and the means of fulfilling desire with the object, between, on the one hand, the experience of satisfaction and the representations to which this experience gives rise and, on the other, the apparatus for obtaining mastery. In its extreme expressions, this corresponds to the ideal form of conjunction found in love and the fatal disjunction of depersonalization. Hatred is not an elemental force; it is one of the passions, a “madness of mastery”. It transforms the inability to find satisfaction with the object into a victory over the object. These views, supported by the analysis of literature, may seem to belong to interpretive psychology more than to metapsychology, but they form a metapsychological theory of our attachment to the object or to objects. By analogy with the way that Freud differentiates, within mental functioning, the pleasure principle and the reality principle, primary and secondary processes, Paul Denis situates the possibility of conjunction or disjunction at another level, that of the sex drive. The notion that “excitation is given form as the drive” implies that sexuality is dependent on the economy of needs (if not that of “the instinct” and “ego instincts”), including attachment relationships and the early communication between mother and child. The drive is constituted through the experience of finding satisfaction with an object, the prototype of which is suckling, the primal seduction. Translated by Boris Wiseman.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Dr Ricardo Bernardi

Abbot Bronstein

Abbot Bronstein

Abbot Bronstein   abbot.bronstein@gmail.com

Dr Bernardi has brought to the attention of readers some of the differences between his view of change in the case of K and those of Dr Feldman. These differences reflect a number of factors which he discusses in terms of the evaluation of change within a psychoanalysis.

The issues raised go well beyond the scope of a letter to the editors and beyond what I, as editor of the Analyst at Work section, can discuss adequately and thoroughly in a short response. I do agree that these are seminal questions and need much fuller discussion and thought and I thank him for raising them both in his letter and his response to the clinical material of Mr K.

One issue that can be addressed here is that of the evidence Dr Bernardi discusses that would result from the analyst's discussion of his own countertransference responses. Although many clinicians find this a valuable way to measure and assess a degree of change in clinical work, both I and the Analyst at Work panel believe, at least for the purpose of this section, that countertransference is a complicated question which can often show a misleading series of data points. We therefore generally ask contributors to only bring in their countertransference reactions when they are very obvious, elicit clear enactments or have become a central part of the analysis as evidenced in the sessions presented. We of course respect the value and importance of countertransference as an indicator of how the analysis and analyst work, but we have found that the analyst's own descriptions of their countertransference vary by clinician, theory and experience and have decided therefore to allow the clinical hours to be more or less free of such added comments. The experience of the readers and discussants then is open to judging the clinical material as it appears in a series of sessions. It is for these reasons that the evidence that Dr Bernardi would have liked included was omitted at the request of the editors.

Dr Bernardi's contention that he did not find evidence of internal change and, as he states, “In my opinion there was an impasse situation that after 10 years of analysis had become immutable,” are strong and definitive statements that are neither the opinion of Dr Feinberg, Dr Feldman or myself. However, this is the central issue to be contended with for all analysts: what constitutes support and evidence of analytic change or psychic change within the analysis itself?

responseSet.title: Panel on “Psychic dimensions of the climate crisis”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel on “Psychic dimensions of the climate crisis”

Adela Abella

Adela Abella

Adela Abella   adela.abella@bluewin.ch

Chair: Donald Moss

Presenters: Delaram Habibi-Kohlen, Luc Magnenat, Sally Weintrobe

The first speaker, Delaram Habibi-Kohlen (German Psychoanalytical Society), gave a paper through a successful teleconference, reporting a series of in-depth interviews conducted in 2011 with psychology students on the topic of “Dealing with climate change”. These interviews and their discussion are impressive illustrations of “how difficult it is to bear the confrontation with the climate catastrophe, and what twists and turns we make in our thinking in order to get out of it”. Some of these methods include: shutting off emotions through intellectualisation and cutting off connections with history or geography in order to isolate the climate crisis from its global context (examples given were that we lack precise forecasting tools, and climate crisis is a threat for some distant islands); refusing to think about its consequences for future generations, presumably in order to prevent guilt and maybe even to satisfy unconscious wishes for Oedipal revenge; and perceiving nature not as damaged by human action but in phantasy as a pre-Oedipal, unpredictable and revengeful mother. In Delaram Habibi-Kohlen’s words:

All in all, people waver between whitewashing (nothing is really so bad, people have always dealt with crises, technology will help us find solutions) and the feeling of being completely apathetic and dependent, helpless and having no power. In between these, one can find feelings of resentment and self-blaming (we don't deserve better/the others don't). A frequent way of dealing with this is to demand the necessary activity exclusively from the politicians, which also means that one can secretly continue to profit from one's own consumption advantages, even if one denounces them.

Luc Magnenat (Swiss Psychoanalytical Society) gave a more theoretical paper in which he suggested understanding the psychical dimensions of climate crisis in terms of a disorder of thought. He first pointed to Harold Searles’ pioneering comments, in 1972, stating the danger of destruction of our world as a consequence of human action. Searles thought the main problem was that the actual damaged state of the world arouses our earliest anxieties while pushing us towards a delusional disavowal. Drawing on the work of Sally Weintrobe (the next and final speaker), Luc Magnenat developed his central point: defence by disavowal implies that “a part of our ego takes note of an excessively distressing or guiltful aspect of reality, while in the same movement another part of our ego defensively denies this aspect of reality”. This particular defensive mode, described by Freud as typical of perversion and melancholy, thought Luc Magnenat, may be better understood in terms of the psychotic part of the personality described by Bion, which bars the way of thinking, understanding and transforming. Thus, his compelling question: “How can we remain transformational, understanding, how can we not become obstructive in the face of data that awaken in us our most fundamental anxieties?” To answer this central question, Luc Magnenat suggested combining listening with action: a listening that allows us to be in contact both with our inner world and with 1029

responseSet.title: A character trait of Freud’s by Joan Riviere (1958)
responseSet.newSectionName: A tribute to Freud on the 80th Anniversary of his Death
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A character trait of Freud’s by Joan Riviere (1958)

Joan Riviere

Joan Riviere

Joan Riviere  

Always one of the most interesting things to me about Freud was his writing; I met him first in his writings before I knew him. You get an impression of the man from them, quite apart from the impression their content makes on you. As is well known his style and presentation are very different from that of most scientific writers. (Actually, Freud's writings do vary and are of more than one description, but I am speaking now of the style which predominates and characterizes the main volume of his work.) Its general character is not only direct and plain-spoken—simple statements without padding— but in particular it conveys vividly an awareness of his readers or hearers, as if he were speaking directly to them, and were concerned to put forward his views in a form intelligible to them. The structure of his argument is not built up in a vacuum, as it were; it has a direct reference to the reader; he is addressing you. There is a personal quality, a personal relation, implicit in his style.

When I came to know Freud himself, however, I found that he did not appear especially interested in impressing himself on people or in seeking to convince others of his views. We know that he needed the support of an outside recognition and the acceptance of this work; he hoped for it, but in everyday life he appeared to take no direct steps to obtain it. There seemed to be a paradox here: on the one hand he had no strong impulse to influence others, to teach or convince them—not even in fact a marked interest in curing them, as he has told us; the aim of impressing himself on people seemed to be lacking or minimal in him. Yet he had developed this special capacity for presenting his conclusions as if he were bent on enabling the reader to take them in—so much so that it colours his whole style and gives the presentation a simplicity and lucidity (often when the content is obscure) that is peculiar to him and most rare in such work.

I wondered about this paradox and why he had this relation to his readers—as if they and their thoughts were an essential factor in the process of formulating his views.

responseSet.title: Freud’s papers on technique and contemporary clinical practice: by Lawrence Friedman, London and New York, Routledge, 2019, 239 pp. $44.95, ISBN: 978-0-8153-8575-2
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud’s papers on technique and contemporary clinical practice

by Lawrence Friedman, London and New York, Routledge, 2019, 239 pp. $44.95, ISBN: 978-0-8153-8575-2

Andrew Druck

Andrew Druck

New York
545 West End Avenue/1A

Andrew Druck   andrew.druck798@gmail.com

Lawrence Friedman has gifted us with an exciting new book, Freud’s Papers on Technique and Contemporary Clinical Practice. The book is primarily a collection of Friedman’s previously published papers, with the addition of a helpful introduction, three orienting prefaces, and a short end section. We have the privilege of following Friedman as he thinks through, with great rigour, depth, and insight, issues inherent to what Friedman sees as Freud's empirical discovery of the “strange” experience that is “the psychoanalytic experience … a novel state of unusual mental freedom” (1; all quotes in this review are from Friedman).

The papers—now chapters—are beautifully written in prose that is non-technical, clear, and suffused with wit. However, they are not always easy reading. They require concentration and thought, as Friedman takes us into a question, leading us as he thinks it through, always considering and evaluating different alternatives that would challenge or modify his main point. Friedman tends to begin with a thesis, based on an aspect of the analytic stance. He develops it, and then moves to questioning it, often based on post-Freudian challenges. He then challenges the challenges, and ends with a conclusion that reaffirms the initial thesis (and Freud's thinking). The papers are masterful contributions, one after the other.

Friedman's focus throughout is on how the analyst's “attitudes” (170) allow the psychoanalytic experience to unfold. He sees Freud's technical recommendations—abstinence, neutrality, and anonymity—as default positions for the analyst, “beacons” (142) within which he can establish and maintain the psychoanalytic phenomena that Freud discovered, and tolerate the necessary ambiguity and paradox that psychoanalysis demands. Friedman writes: “It bears repeating that most of those anti-analytic attitudes (the ‘don’t’s) that Freud's Papers on Technique cautions against are normal social attitudes. It is the task of training to make what is perfectly normal feel inappropriate to analysts while they are at work” (224).

responseSet.title: Relational conundrums: A critique of Contemporary Psychoanalysis by Jon Mills
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Relational conundrums: A critique of Contemporary Psychoanalysis by Jon Mills

Eyal Rozmarin

Eyal Rozmarin

USA

Eyal Rozmarin   eyal.rozmarin@gmail.com

Relational Psychoanalysis is by now a large and influential international psychoanalytic school with its own association and publications. For the joy of some and the lament or indifference of others, it is a significant vein of contemporary psychoanalytic discourse. Relational thought has modified and added some ideas to psychoanalysis as it has marginalized and dismissed others. To some, it has saved psychoanalysis from its more retrograde tendencies, to others it has betrayed its very essence. The contributions of the relational school and its place in the history and opus of psychoanalysis, its influences, appeal and rigour, are all interesting questions to contemplate. And so we should thank Jon Mills for having written a book that attempts to address these questions, a book that is a rare effort to consider the grand ideas and narratives that underlie our discipline. This book forces the reader to engage with the most basic questions about subjectivity, experience, knowledge and the very world we live in, as individuals and as psychoanalysts who chose the deliberation of meaning-making as their mission. And it does so while conversing with philosophical traditions whose relations to psychoanalysis are of the essence, a mission that is also of great value.

Mills introduces his project as an appreciative, constructive and above all rigorous critique of relational psychoanalysis. Yet already in the book’s very first pages, one encounters a rather uncritical tendency to render complex, nuanced ideas in simplistic, sometimes rather distorted fashion. The first of these instances, one on which Mills’ critique draws significantly and repeatedly, is his formulation of the relationship between relational theory and the concept of the unconscious. Already in the preface, Mills makes a sweeping claim: relational psychoanalysis 166

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The drive as paradigm—Laplanche’s sexual as paradigm shift

Richard Simpson

Richard Simpson

⁣Membre habilité, La sociétié psychanalytique de Montréal

Richard Simpson   CONTACT Richard B. Simpson richardbsimpson@me.com 1064 avenue Bernard, Suite 50 Outremont, Québec, H2V 1V2 Canada

Drive, Paradigm, Laplanche, Agamben, Instinct

This paper begins with the argument that Freud's concept of the drive can be viewed epistemologically as a “paradigm” using the definition developed by Agamben. A paradigm is an analogical form of knowledge where the grouping of a set of examples makes phenomena more intelligible without there being a general rule for membership in the group. Next, this view of the drive as paradigm is used to argue that Laplanche's development of his concept of “le sexual” in French, rendered in English as the sexual [in italics], is a paradigm shift for Anglophone psychoanalysis. Laplanche's distinction in Freud's German text between Trieb and Instinkt makes clear the confusion caused for Anglophones by Strachey's relentless translation of Trieb as “instinct” in the Standard Edition. The essence of Freud's infantile sexuality is connected to Laplanche's view that the drive is sexuality beyond genitality, beyond sexual difference which is extremely mobile as to aim and object with an economic regime in the pursuit of excitation. The drive is the source of enigmatic messages left by the child's caretakers and forms the unconscious. Ramifications of Laplanche's view of the drive are explored in a clinical case from the literature and in a film by Kieslowski.

L'auteur soutient d'abord la thèse que le concept Freudien de la pulsion peut être considéré épistémologiquement comme un «paradigme» selon la définition développée par Agamben. Un paradigme est une forme analogique de compréhension où un groupement collectif d'exemples rend les phénomènes plus intelligibles sans règle générale pour l'appartenance au groupe. Cette vision de la pulsion comme paradigme est ensuite utilisée pour démontrer que le développement par Laplanche de son concept français du sexual, traduit en anglais comme the sexual (en italiques), est un changement de paradigme pour la psychanalyse anglophone. La distinction que fait Laplanche dans le texte allemand de Freud entre Trieb et Instinkt démontre clairement la confusion crée pour les anglophones par la traduction persistante de Trieb comme « instinct » par Strachey dans le Standard Edition. L'essence de la sexualité infantile de Freud est liée à l'idée de Laplanche selon laquelle la pulsion est une sexualité au-delà du stade génital et de la différence sexuelle, qui est exceptionnellement mobile quant à ses fins et ses objets au sein d'un régime économique qui ne poursuit que l'excitation. Les messages énigmatiques provenant des pulsions des gardiens de l'enfant sont la source de son inconscient. Les ramifications de cette conception laplanchienne des pulsions sont explorées dans une cure psychanalytique et dans un film de Kieslowski.

Dieser Beitrag beginnt mit der These, dass Freuds Triebbegriff nach der Definition von Agamben epistemologisch als „Paradigma“ gesehen werden kann. Ein Paradigma ist eine analoge Wissensform, bei der die Gruppierung einer Reihe von Beispielen Phänomene verständlicher macht, ohne dass es eine allgemeine Regel für die Zugehörigkeit zu dieser Gruppe gibt. Im Anschluss wird anhand dieser Sicht von Trieb als Paradigma argumentiert, dass Laplanches Entwicklung seines Begriffs „le sexual“ im Französischen – in der englischen Übersetzung the sexual – einen Paradigmenwechsel in der englischsprachigen Psychoanalyse darstellt. Laplanches Unterscheidung zwischen Trieb und Instinkt in Freuds deutschsprachigem Text verdeutlicht die Verwirrung, die in der englischsprachigen Welt durch Stracheys unablässige Übersetzung von Trieb mit „instinct“ in der Standardausgabe der Werke von Freud gestiftet wurde. Das Wesen kindlicher Sexualität nach Freud steht in Verbindung mit Laplanches Auffassung von Trieb als Sexualität jenseits der Genitalität und jenseits der Geschlechterdifferenz, der hinsichtlich des Ziels und des Objekts ausgesprochen beweglich ist und beim Streben nach Erregung nach einem ökonomischem System handelt. Der Trieb ist die Quelle rätselhafter Botschaften, die von jenen hinterlassen werden, die für ein Kind sorgen, und daraus wird das Unbewusste gestaltet. Verzweigungen von Laplanches Sicht des Triebs werden anhand eines klinischen Fallbeispiels aus der Literatur und eines Films von Kieslowski untersucht.

Il presente articolo sostiene in prima battuta che il concetto freudiano di pulsione può essere considerato in una prospettiva epistemologica come “paradigma” nel senso che a questa parola dà Agamben. Un paradigma è una forma analogica di conoscenza per cui il raggruppamento di un insieme di esempi aiuta a rendere i fenomeni più comprensibili anche in assenza di una regola generale che stabilisca i criteri dell'appartenenza al gruppo. L'idea che la pulsione rappresenti in questo senso un paradigma viene poi usata per sostenere che lo sviluppo del concetto di “le sexual” (in francese) da parte di Laplanche, tradotto in inglese come ‘the sexual', rappresenta un cambio di paradigma per la psicoanalisi anglofona. La distinzione che Laplanche mantiene tra i termini Trieb e Instinkt del testo freudiano tedesco chiarisce infatti la confusione causata per gli anglofoni dalla traduzione di Strachey, che nella Standard Edition traduce costantemente Trieb con la parola “instinct”. L'essenza della sessualità infantile per come la intendeva Freud viene qui collegata all'idea di Laplanche per cui la pulsione sarebbe sessualità operante al di là della genitalità e pure al di là di una differenza sessuale che è estremamente mobile quanto a obiettivo e a oggetto: una sessualità, peraltro, caratterizzata da un regime di tipo economico nella sua ricerca dell'eccitamento. La pulsione è fonte di messaggi enigmatici lasciati dai caregiver del bambino e dà forma all'inconscio. L’articolo esamina anche le ramificazioni delle idee di Laplanche a proposito della pulsione, commentando un caso clinico tratto dalla letteratura analitica e un film di Kieslowski.

El presente artículo empieza con el argumento de que el concepto de pulsión de Freud puede considerarse epistemológicamente como un paradigma, siguiendo la definición de Agamben. Un paradigma es una forma analógica de conocimiento, donde el agrupamiento de un conjunto de ejemplos hace que los fenómenos sean más inteligibles, sin que haya una regla general para ser miembro del grupo. Luego se usa esta visión de la pulsión como paradigma a fin de argumentar que el concepto le sexual, en francés, traducido al inglés como the sexual, desarrollado por Laplanche, es un cambio de paradigma para el psicoanálisis anglófono. La distinción efectuada por Laplanche entre Trieb e Instinkt en el texto de Freud, en alemán, aclara la confusión que causó Strachey en los anglófonos al traducir incesantemente el término Trieb como instinto, en la Standard Edition. La esencia de la sexualidad infantil de Freud está conectada con la visión de Laplanche de que la pulsión es sexualidad más allá de la genitalidad, de la diferencia de sexos, que es extremadamente móvil respecto al fin y al objeto, con un régimen económico en la búsqueda de excitación. La pulsión es la fuente de mensajes enigmáticos dejados por los cuidadores del niño o niña, y forma el inconsciente. Se exploran las ramificaciones de la visión de pulsión de Laplanche en un caso clínico de la literatura y en una película de Kieslowski.

[[RunningHead]]

Desire that enjoys mourning [Il desiderio che ama il lutto] by Sarantis Thanopulos

Patrizia Cupelloni

Patrizia Cupelloni

Roma
Italy

Patrizia Cupelloni   patriziacupelloni@gmail.com

This work by Sarantis Thanopulos, which has a very appealing title, comes across as a sort of passionate defence of some new perspectives that could make psychoanalysis itself an object of desire if they were to deal bravely with illusion and loss. The discipline of psychoanalysis owes its vitality to research and the awareness that it is degraded and demeaned by clinging to dogma.

It is a short book; rich and original, which is articulated around the theme of lack, understood as the guarantor of all difference. Thanopulos delves into the theoretical core of identification and, in particular, hysterical identification, which he distinguishes from and compares with narcissistic identification. These are some of the central themes concerning the intersection and connection between hysteria and identification that I found in my reading. Beyond Freud, but starting from Freud, Thanopulos focuses on the theme of desire and its object. Freud describes desire as a drive, different from the satisfaction of a need, searching in vain for the primary object that has been lost forever. The source of desire can thus be traced back to a replacement of the original satisfaction at the breast, which is no longer present in the perception of the real primary object but is achieved instead through a hallucinatory retention of it. This transition allows for its recovery and, through the first perception, its reappearance.

157

responseSet.title: “La psychosomatique” [Psychosomatics], Débats en psychanalyse edited by Félicie Nayrou and Gérard Szwec
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“La psychosomatique” [Psychosomatics], Débats en psychanalyse edited by Félicie Nayrou and Gérard Szwec

Kalyane Fejtö

Kalyane Fejtö

Paris
France

Kalyane Fejtö   kalyanefejto@gmail.com

Débats en psychanalyse has dedicated an issue, edited by Félicie Nayrou and Gérard Szwec, to a specific theoretico-clinical field: the psychoanalytic psychosomatics that emerged with the Paris School of Psychosomatics in the 1950s and 1960s. As Szwec emphasises in his 173 introduction, although the links between the psyche and the soma have been explored by Freud and his successors (in particular Ferenczi and Groddeck), the psychosomatics of the Paris School has considered the psychic functioning of somatic patients from a new angle that the various articles in this volume help to illuminate from their contemporary perspective.

Historically, the psychosomatics of the Paris School was created by psychoanalysts who were members of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society, principally: Pierre Marty, Michel Fain, Michel de M'Uzan and Christian David. Based on their analytic training rooted in Freudian metapsychology, the first generation of psychosomaticians took an interest in somatic illness and some of them worked in that capacity in medical services. This is attested by the historical texts at the end of the book, consisting of interventions by Marty, Fain and de M'Uzan at a surgery conference in 1959, which report their work in their psychosomatics service in a general surgery department.

The interest in somatic illness nevertheless led psychosomaticians to suggest a very different clinical theorisation from the nosological frameworks of medicine because they highlighted the patients’ psychic functioning rather than the illness from which they were suffering. Moreover, they rejected the possibility of using the model of neurosis that considers symptoms in terms of their unconscious meaning. As a result, they carried out a reversal of perspective in relation to the “psychosomatic medicine” of Franz Alexander (Chicago School) which, being based on the model of the hysterical conversion, sought to demonstrate a causal action between psychological factors and physiological processes. The creation in 1972 of an institute independent of the SPP (Société psychanalytique du Paris (Paris psychoanalitical society)) – IPSO (the Institute of Psychosomatics) – arose from the need for a specific training based on a new theoretical model that involved rethinking the setting and introducing specific forms of therapy.

responseSet.title: Introduction to “On the value of the Lacanian approach to analytic practice”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to “On the value of the Lacanian approach to analytic practice”

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass   CONTACT Rachel B. Blass RachelBlass@googlemail.com

Dialogue with analysts who belong to analytic schools other than our own allows us a better understanding of those schools. It helps to clarify misconceptions we may have regarding others, discern where assumed differences are merely terminological and where they are essential, and better appreciate the grounds of the essential ones. It helps us locate ourselves relative to others, which is especially important when we share analytic institutions and other frameworks. Also, more in the spirit of controversy, it allows us to explain our views to our rivals and to express our objections to theirs, perhaps with the hope of influencing those who are open to change. But also significant is the fact that such dialogue invites us to better define and understand our own analytic thinking and practice. As it requires of us to articulate these to other analysts who are not like-minded and to reflect on these from their eyes, to think in terms not only of what we do maintain and practise but also what we don’t, our view of our own positions may be deepened and enriched and may consequently also further evolve and change.

The present controversy involves a dialogue between Lacanian and IPA non-Lacanian analysts. One reason that this dialogue is of special interest is that despite the great diversity of analytic approaches within the IPA, for many decades Lacanian psychoanalysis has developed outside of it. Is Lacanian psychoanalysis indeed so fundamentally different from the range of approaches that are prevalent within the IPA today that the physical separation is indeed still meaningful, not just a vestige of the past?

Developing a dialogue on this topic is not an easy task, in part because of a noted gap between Lacanian and non-Lacanian analytic language, and style of discourse, with Lacanians tending to be more abstract and theoretical than non-Lacanians (see Borossa, Bronstein, and Pajaczkowska 2015; Morgan 2018). As a result, for many non-Lacanian analysts, Lacanian writings are obscure. To promote dialogue, this obstacle must be dealt with. To this end, we organized a focused and clinically oriented direct exchange between a Lacanian analyst and several non-Lacanian analysts. We felt that it would be important that the Lacanian would be someone able to convey his thinking and practice and clinically illustrate it in a way that would be meaningful to analysts who are non-Lacanians and may have only limited familiarity with Lacan. For this role we chose Bruce Fink. Fink is an American practising Lacanian psychoanalyst, trained in France, and a member of the École de la Cause freudienne in Paris. He is a major translator of Lacan and author of many books on Lacanian psychoanalysis, which are well known for their clarity and accessibility to non-Lacanians as well as for their discussion of clinical issues, alongside theoretical ones. 311

responseSet.title: Response to Bruce Fink
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Bruce Fink

Sara Flanders

Sara Flanders

Sara Flanders   CONTACT Sara Flanders sarajflanders@gmail.com 12 Caedmon Road, London, N7 6DH

Bruce Fink offers an open and clear exposition of his Americanized Lacan, which is to say, Lacan without the “preciosity” and opaqueness that has in the past driven many analysts into defensive resistance to his major contribution to psychoanalytic thought. There is almost no moment when one feels reading Fink that we do not know what he is talking about. However, he does not give us a session in vivo, so we are more or less left to surmise what he might say in any given session. About his perspective as analyst, however, he leaves us in no doubt. And as for his Lacanian perspective on psychoanalysis, there is no doubt in my mind that he has something to say to all psychoanalysts. The difficulty lies in sifting out what can be useful, what, on examination, represents a limitation in the psychoanalytic field he portrays, and what seems downright antithetical to the best practice of psychoanalysis.

Fink acknowledges his Americanization of Lacan. American too is the context of his comparative study. He seems to be speaking primarily, even exclusively, of American psychoanalysis when he writes of psychoanalysis today. Unlike Lacan, who took on as his theoretical adversaries the distinguished ego psychologists of his day—Hartman, Kris, Lowenstein, the latter his first analyst—Fink addresses himself primarily to an anonymous and, it seems to me, in his one extended example, a relatively weak psychoanalyst, poorly grounded in understanding of the frame and the process. It is a psychoanalysis that has diminished into a kind of psychotherapy that even he acknowledges is not representative of non-Lacanian psychoanalysis: he hypothesizes alternative non-Lacanian interpretations that might be agreed upon by the psychoanalytic community at large in order to debate technique with the clinician he critiques. Straw men abound in this paper, which may be a result of the limitation of Fink’s exposure to contemporary psychoanalysis in the US. More ominously, Fink may be addressing a pervasive dwindling of psychoanalysis into an approximate sort of psychotherapy, a field that is yearly losing ground, discipline, and knowledge. He describes having criticized the work of Owen Renik, Thomas Ogden, Patrick Casement and Ralph Greenson, certainly not a homogeneous group; one would have to tailor a critique to all of them differently. However, he does not single out these distinguished analysts when he calls attention to what he regards as the failures of contemporary non-Lacanian analysis. Nor does he offer much of his own work as example. There are his views, which are very interesting, sometimes illuminating, and the bad analysis he criticizes.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Id follows: It Follows (2014) and the existential crisis of adolescent sexuality

Joseph Barbera

Joseph Barbera

Toronto
Canada
The Department of Psychiatry

Joseph Barbera   CONTACT Joseph Barbera joseph.barbera@utoronto.ca Youthdale Treatment Centres, 227 Victoria St. LL 2, Toronto, ON M5B 1T8, Canada

Introduction

It Follows is a critically acclaimed independent horror film, written and directed by David Robert Mitchell. This groundbreaking film pays homage to slasher films of the past while at the same time rejuvenating this otherwise tired genre with a complex narrative and visual aesthetic that makes it an original film in its own right. The movie centres around Jay, a young teenager, and her attempts to evade an unnamed entity that is transmitted from victim to victim through sexual intercourse. The film has been interpreted in various ways, including as a parable for HIV (Lyne 2015) and as a film about teenage sexual anxiety (Parkin 2015). The film, however, contains a depth and complexity of material that leaves it open to a richer interpretation, informed by psychoanalytic theories of unconscious experience and adolescent sexuality.

Film synopsis

The film opens on a peaceful suburban neighbourhood. A young woman dressed only in sleepwear runs frantically out of her house, looks around the street in terror, and to the bewilderment of her father, drives away into the night. The next morning we see her lying dead on a beach, her right leg sticking up into the air and hyperextended nearly ninety degrees at the knee, as if subjected to an inhuman force.

In the next scene we see Jay, a 19-year-old woman, floating peacefully in an above-ground pool in her backyard. Subsequent scenes illustrate Jay's typical suburban life, shared with her sister Kelly, and friends Yara and Paul. Jay goes on a date with a young man, Hugh, whom she has recently met. During the date Hugh leads Jay abruptly out of a movie theatre after seeing a girl in a yellow dress whom Jay herself cannot see. On a subsequent date, parked behind an abandoned building, Jay and Hugh have intercourse for the first time in the back of Hugh's car. While Jay lies in the back of Hugh's car, Hugh renders her unconscious by putting a rag over her mouth. Jay awakens, still dressed only in her underwear, tied to a wheelchair in the parking garage of the abandoned building. Hugh's intent is not to harm Jay but to warn her about “this thing” which he relates is going to follow her, an entity that someone gave to him and which he passed to her in the car, i.e. during the act of intercourse. He further relates that “It” can take on various forms, including strangers or loved ones, in order to get close to you. The only way to get rid of It is to pass it along to someone else by having sex with them. 393

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transformational processes in clinical psychoanalysis: Dreaming, emotions and the present moment

by Lawrence J. Brown, Routledge, 2018, 250 pp. £29.99. ISBN: 9781138323926

Chris Mawson

Chris Mawson

London

Chris Mawson   cpsmawson@gmail.com

The subject of Lawrence Brown’s book is transformation, as the concept pertains to psychoanalytic practice. With modesty from the outset, he states that by limiting his field of study to transformative processes discernible in clinical work he has been helped to forego an omnipotent 619 task, a definitive study of the concept that would, he says, become a preposterous over-reaching. Accordingly, Brown proceeds—after a useful introduction to the structure of his book and to some basic assumptions underlying his project—by employing a range of concepts, some derived from Bion’s own work on psychic transformation, some developed from his own clinical and theoretical contributions and from those colleagues whose emphasis accords closely with his own. The interaction of these perspectives, their breadth in particular, but also their clinical and aesthetic dimensions, makes for a fascinatingly pluralistic investigation. Like Bion before him, Brown’s intellectual curiosity for investigating the intersections between learning and growth is clear, and his work on the nature of transformation in the clinical practice of psychoanalysis is a serious contribution in its own right, as well as a welcome addition to a consideration of Bion’s later clinical thinking, in which his emphasis shifted from epistemology, a model of thinking, towards mental growth, while retaining both sets of ideas. That concern—most specifically the shift noticeable in chapter 11 of the 1965 work—had developed largely from discussions between Bion and Betty Joseph in the mid-1960s, and in my view, Brown’s book can usefully be read in conjunction both with Joseph’s (1989) Psychic Equilibrium and Psychic Change and with Feldman’s work (2009) on the relationship between doubt and conviction in the practice of psychoanalysis.

Bion had hoped that his 1965 book on transformations (the subtitle of which was: Change from Learning to Growth) would have been complete in itself but soon realised that reading it profitably required the infrastructure of the conceptual “ladders” developed in Learning from Experience and Elements of Psychoanalysis, even though some of the concepts had undergone a few transformations of their own in his thinking over the intervening years.

by Salman Akhtar, New York, Routledge, 2018, €26.99, 233 pp. ISBN: 978-1782200673

Michael Moskowitz

Michael Moskowitz

Michael Moskowitz   moskowitz.michael@gmail.com

It is hard to overestimate the contributions that Salman Akhtar has made to our understanding of cultural factors in personality. Starting with his groundbreaking 1995 paper, “A Third Individuation: Immigration, Identity, and the Psychoanalytic Process,” Akhtar has been steadfast in trying to show the psychoanalytic world that culture and history matter, and in particular the ways in which cultural transformations impact the psyche. A major contributor to the rising sea of challenges to the white-is-normative assumption of North American psychoanalysis, Akhtar has produced a steady stream of books, both authored and edited, and journal 801

responseSet.title: Madeleine Baranger
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Madeleine Baranger

Luis Kancyper

Luis Kancyper

Luis Kancyper   CONTACT Luis Kancyper kancyperl@gmail.com

Madeleine Baranger, who passed away on 19 June 2017 at the age of 97, was born in France in 1920. A graduate of Classical Studies at the University of Toulouse (1941), she was a member of the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association and the International Psychoanalytical Association since 1959. Between 1954 and 1965, she and Willy Baranger founded the Uruguayan Psychoanalytical Association, organising the Institute of Psychoanalysis, where she was Director from 1955 to 1964, collaborating with the Revista Uruguaya de Psicoanálisis (Uruguayan Journal of Psychoanalysis), and acting as a training analyst, supervisor, and professor. She continued these activities at the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association upon her return to Buenos Aires in 1966, always maintaining her anti-authoritarian, anti-dogmatic, and anti-Pygmalion position as a champion of tolerance and respect for the other and for the creative potential within each subject. She received the Konex Award in 1996 and the international Sigourney Award in 2008.

Her work can be seen as a series of investigations on a range of thematic clusters related to the theory of technique, psychopathology, and the impact of the ethical and ideological on the analytic task.

She authored papers presented at several Congresses of the International Psychoanalytical Association, as well as books and articles published in Spanish and translated into French, English, Italian, and Turkish.

She published numerous papers in prestigious psychoanalytic journals, including the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association's Revista de Psicoanálisis (Journal of Psychoanalysis), and the Journal of the Uruguayan Psychoanalytic Association.

Madé's entire body of work serves as a call to broaden the borders of psychoanalysis and to keep alive the promise of its galvanising, questioning power. Her manner of participating, more than giving answers, was to pose questions. This is the most poignant aspect of her work: its questioning.

responseSet.title: Report on the panel: “Did Bion change his mind? Three phases of his clinical approach”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Report on the panel: “Did Bion change his mind? Three phases of his clinical approach”

Rodrigo Barahona

Rodrigo Barahona

Rodrigo Barahona   rodbarahona@gmail.com

Chair: Joseph Aguayo (Los Angeles)

Presenters: Sira Dermen (London), Robert Hinshelwood (Wisbech), Bernet Malin (Santa Monica)

Reporter: Rodrigo Barahona

Dr Robert Hinshelwood started the panel off by describing the influence of Wilfred Trotter early on in Bion's career. Trotter, who was also Ernest Jones's brother-in-law, probably influenced Bion in the direction of psychotherapy. Although Bion qualified as a doctor in 1930, Dr Hinshelwood told the audience that, prior to 1941, there is very little on record to help us assess what Bion was like as a therapist.

Despite Bion's scorn for authority, Dr Hinshelwood said, he much admired Trotter's “unassuming capacity to listen,” which became the basis for his later ideas on reverie and the core to his approach. While working with the War Office Selection Boards during World War II, Bion was reading Kurt Lewin and was inspired by his social field theory. In addition to his respect for maintaining an adequate level of uncertainty when listening to groups of soldiers, he picked up an appreciation for the interaction between the individual applicant for selection as an officer and the context in which he was placed. John Rickman's encouragement here and during the Northfield Experiment in 1943 was crucial in the sense of how he understood the interview process and the psychoanalytic situation in field theory terms. According to Dr Hinshelwood, for Rickman the first requirement of the analyst was to learn to accept any role the patient puts upon him. Dr Hinshelwood explained that, from Rickman's approach, a set of principles could be derived, which may be thought of as having influenced Bion, although the latter rarely spoke directly about field theory: (1) subjective introspection by the analyst; (2) reflection by the analyst on that experience; (3) the formulation of their experience in terms of the role in which the analyst feels they are cast; and (4) the roles forming a narrative content of an implicit drama in the group that both are playing out.

responseSet.authorMast: Marianna Ferreira Jorge and  Paula Sibilia
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Online “Addiction” as a Malaise of the 21st Century: From repression by the law to “free” unlimited stimulation

Marianna Jorge and Paula Sibilia

Paula Sibilia

Niterói
Brazil

Paula Sibilia   CONTACT Paula Sibilia paulasibilia@gmail.com
Suffering 1, Connection 2, Subjectivity 3, Repression 4, Limits 5

This article proposes to explore the notion of “civilization malaise”, or discontent, as defined by Sigmund Freud in 1930, focusing on the challenges posed by the “addictive” use of digital communication and information technologies. Using the genealogical perspective, the hypothesis is that in the last decades a great historical transformation is happening, which affects subjectivities and ways of suffering. The obedient citizens of industrial society, repressed by the rigor of the law under “bourgeois morality” or “protestant ethics”, asphyxiated by the solid institutional walls and by the rigidity of the modern “iron cage” would be losing prominence. In their place, hyperstimulated and self-centered consumers proliferate, governed more by desire and self-realization than by duty and guilt. The insistent connection to digital networks, voluntary although difficult to (self) control is a paradigmatic case to study these changes.

Cet article propose d'explorer la notion de « malaise de culture », ainsi définie par Sigmund Freud en 1930, en se concentrant sur les défis posés par l'utilisation « addictive » des technologies numériques de la communication et de l’informatique. En utilisant la perspective généalogique, l'hypothèse propose que, dans les dernières décennies, une grande transformation historique s’est produite, influençant les subjectivités et les modes de souffrance. Les citoyens obéissants de la société industrielle, étouffés par la rigueur de la loi, elle-même soumise à la « morale bourgeoise » ou l'« éthique protestante », asphyxiés par des cloisons institutionnels rigides et par l’ankylose de la « cage de fer » moderne, perdraient leur primauté. À leur place, se multiplient les consommateurs hyperstimulés et égocentriques, gouvernés plus par le désir et la réalisation de soi que par le devoir et la culpabilité. La connexion incessante aux réseaux informatiques, volontaire mais difficilement réglée par le self-control, est un cas paradigmatique pour l’étude de tels changements.

Dieser Artikel regt dazu an, das “Unbehagen in der Kultur“ zu erforschen, wie es von Sigmund Freud 1930 definiert wurde und dabei den Schwerpunkt bei den Herausforderungen zu setzen, die aus der „süchtig machenden“ Formen der Nutzung digitaler Kommunikation und Informationstechnologien erwachsen. Aus genealogischer Sicht ergibt sich die Hypothese, dass in den letzten Jahrzehnten eine große historische Umwälzung in Gang gesetzt wurde, von der Subjektivitäten und Arten des Leidens beeinflusst werden. Die gehorsamen Bürger der Industriegesellschaft, die von der Unerbittlichkeit des Gesetzes unter dem Einfluss „kleinbürgerlicher Moral“ oder „protestantischer Ethik“ unterdrückt wurden – erstickt durch die massiven institutionellen Mauern und die Starrheit des modernen „eisernen Käfigs“ – würden an Bedeutung verlieren. An deren Stelle treten immer mehr übermäßig stimulierte und ichbezogene Konsumenten, die stärker von ihrem Begehren und dem Wunsch nach Selbstverwirklichung gelenkt werden als von Pflicht- und Schuldgefühlen. Die ständige Verbindung mit digitalen Netzwerken, die freiwillig ist, aber schwer (selbst) beherrscht werden kann, ist ein paradigmatischer Fall zur Untersuchung dieser Veränderungen.

Questo articolo propone di esaminare il concetto di “disagio della civiltà”, secondo la definizione freudiana del 1930, concentrandosi sulle sfide poste dalla dipendenza dall’uso della comunicazione e delle tecnologie digitali. L’ipotesi principale, formulata utilizzando la prospettiva genealogica, è che in questi ultimi decenni sia in corso una grande trasformazione storica il cui impatto interessa sia le soggettività sia le modalità della sofferenza. I cittadini obbedienti tipici della società industriale, repressi dal rigore della legge sotto l’egida della “moralità borghese” o dell’“etica protestante” e oltre a ciò asfissiati dalle solide mura delle istituzioni e dalla rigidità della moderna “gabbia di ferro”, starebbero passando in secondo piano. E quel che si trova al loro posto è una proliferazione di consumatori iperstimolati e autocentrati, governati più dal desiderio e dall’autorealizzazione che dal dovere e dalla colpa. La connessione perenne ai network digitali, volontaria per quanto difficile da (auto)controllare, si presta come caso paradigmatico per lo studio di questi cambiamenti.

Este artículo se propone explorar la idea de “malestar en la cultura”, como la definió Sigmund Freud en 1930, y se centra en los desafíos planteados por el uso “adictivo” de las tecnologías de la información y la comunicación digitales. A partir de una perspectiva genealógica, se presenta la hipótesis de que en las últimas décadas se está dando una gran transformación histórica, que afecta las subjetividades y los modos de sufrimiento. Los ciudadanos obedientes de la sociedad industrial, reprimidos por el rigor de la ley bajo la “moralidad burguesa” o la “ética protestante”, y asfixiados por los sólidos muros institucionales y por la rigidez de la “jaula de hierro” moderna, estarían perdiendo prominencia. En su lugar, proliferan consumidores hiperestimulados y egocéntricos, gobernados más por el deseo y la autorrealización que por el deber y la culpa. La conexión insistente a redes digitales, voluntaria aunque difícil de (auto)controlar, es un caso paradigmático para el estudio de estos cambios.

responseSet.title: Some brief personal reflections on the 100th Anniversary Conference papers. Where are we? Where have we come from? Where might we go?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some brief personal reflections on the 100th Anniversary Conference papers. Where are we? Where have we come from? Where might we go?

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

London
UK

David Tuckett   CONTACT David Tuckett d.tuckett@ucl.ac.uk University College, London, UK; Institute of Psychoanalysis, UK

This contribution is a personal reflection on the papers presented at the New York and London anniversary conferences, in which the author takes stock to consider what they might tell us about where the discipline is now, where it has come from and where it might go. Taking the clinical contributions as evidence of the way many leading analysts in the field now proceed, it is suggested that there is an increasing and more and more subtle use of the analyst's behaviour, thoughts, fantasies, feelings and experiences, inside and outside the session, as core sources for constructing the patient's unconscious. Placing this observation in the context of issues of evidence raised in the 50th and 75th anniversary volumes, the author argues that a possible future agenda for the field may be to think more plainly about the basis of an analyst's knowledge claims, whether made implicitly or explicitly to patients in sessions. The author wonders if more disciplined understanding of transference consistent with neuroscientific findings of the last few years may be one factor that could prove useful.

Cette contribution est une réflexion personnelle sur les présentations données aux conférences du 100ème anniversaire à New York et à Londres, dans lesquelles l'auteur fait le point sur le passé, le présent et le futur de la profession. Considérant les contributions cliniques comme représentatifs de la façon dont nombreux psychanalystes de premier plan procèdent actuellement, l'auteur suggère qu'il y a une utilisation croissante, et de plus en plus subtile, du comportement de l'analyste, ses pensées, fantasmes, sentiments et expériences, dans et en dehors de la séance, comme source primaire de construction de l'inconscient du patient. Examinant la manière dont ce même thème était soulevé dans les volumes des 50ème et 75ème anniversaires par rapport à une problématique de preuves, l'auteur entretient l'idée qu'un éventuel plan futur pour la discipline pourrait consister à réfléchir plus profondément sur les assertions des psychanalystes, qu'elles soient faites implicitement ou explicitement aux patients en séance. L'auteur se demande si une compréhension plus disciplinée du transfert, alignée avec les résultats neuroscientifiques des dernières années, pourrait fournir un élément utile.

Dieser Beitrag ist eine persönliche Betrachtung der Vorträge bei den Konferenzen in New York und London anlässlich des 100. Jahrestages – eine Bestandsaufnahme des Autors, um daräber nachzudenken, was sie uns darüber mitteilen könnten, wo sich die Disziplin derzeit befindet, woher sie kommt und wohin sie gehen könnte. Nimmt man die klinischen Beiträge als Beleg dafür, in welche Richtung sich viele führende praktizierende Analytiker derzeit bewegen, so deuten sie darauf hin, dass vermehrt und immer subtiler Verhalten, Gedanken, Fantasien, Gefühle und das Erleben des Analytikers inner- und außerhalb der Sitzung als zentrale Quellen für die Entwicklung des Unbewussten des Patienten genutzt werden. Der Autor ordnet diese Beobachtung in den Kontext der Frage nach der Beweisbarkeit ein, die in den zum 50. und 75. Jahrestag erschienen Bänden zur Sprache kam und argumentiert, dass eine künftige Agenda für das Fachgebiet sein könnte, deutlicher über die Grundlage des Wissensanspruchs von Analytikern nachzudenken, ob er nun implizit oder explizit während der Sitzungen gegenüber Patienten geltend gemacht wird. Der Autor fragt sich, ob ein disziplinierteres Verständnis von Übertragung, das mit neurowissenschaftlichen Erkenntnissen der letzten Jahre vereinbar ist, ein Faktor sein könnte, der sich als nützlich erweisen kann.

Questo scritto propone delle considerazioni personali sui lavori presentati alle conferenze organizzate a New York e a Londra per celebrare il centesimo anniversario della Rivista. Tirando le somme, l'autore riflette su che cosa i vari contributi possano dirci rispetto alla posizione in cui oggi si trova la psicoanalisi, sul passato da cui essa proviene e sulle possibili strade future. Trattando i vari contributi clinici come rappresentazioni paradigmatiche del modo in cui molti dei più accreditati analisti lavorano oggi, viene sottolineato l'uso sempre più frequente e raffinato del comportamento, dei pensieri, delle fantasie, dei sentimenti e delle esperienze dell'analista (sia in seduta sia fuori) quali fonti imprescindibili per la costruzione dell'inconscio del paziente. Inserendo questa osservazione nel contesto in cui, tanto nel numero del cinquantesimo quanto in quello del settantacinquesimo anniversario, veniva sollevato il problema delle evidenze, L'Autore sostiene che un possibile tema di riflessione per la disciplina potrebbe essere in futuro quello di pensare in maniera più semplice e concreta a quali siano le basi su cui poggia la conoscenza dell'analista, a prescindere dal fatto che tale conoscenza venga poi rivendicata implicitamente o esplicitamente con i pazienti in seduta. L'Autore si domanda se una comprensione del transfert più strutturata e in linea con le scoperte fatte negli ultimi anni nel campo delle neuroscienze non possa rivelarsi un fattore utile nell'ambito di queste ricerch.

Esta contribución es una reflexión personal sobre los artículos presentados en los encuentros por el aniversario de esta revista en Nueva York y Londres, en la que el autor hace un balance para considerar qué podrían decirnos estos acerca de dónde está hoy la disciplina, de dónde viene y hacia dónde podría ir. Tomando los aportes clínicos como evidencia de la manera en que proceden hoy muchos analistas destacados en el campo, se sugiere que hay un uso cada vez más sutil de las conductas, fantasías, sentimientos y experiencias del analista, dentro y fuera de la sesión, como fuente central para la construcción del inconsciente del paciente. El autor coloca esta observación en el contexto de las cuestiones de evidencia planteadas en los volúmenes en ocasión del 50 y 75 aniversario de esta revista, y sostiene que una agenda futura para el campo podría ser pensar con mucho más claridad acerca de los fundamentos de las pretensiones de conocimiento de los analistas, sean estas formuladas de modo implícito o explícito a los pacientes en las sesiones. El autor se pregunta si podría resultar útil una comprensión más disciplinada de la transferencia, congruente con los hallazgos neurocientíficos de los últimos años.

responseSet.title: Comments on B.H. Clarke's paper: A cat is not a battleship: thoughts on the meaning of “neuropsychoanalysis”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Comments on B.H. Clarke's paper: A cat is not a battleship: thoughts on the meaning of “neuropsychoanalysis”

Larry Sandberg

Larry Sandberg

New York
NY
205 East 78th Street 1A

Larry Sandberg   lsandbergmd@mac.com

To the editor:

I read with interest B.H. Clarke's paper, “A Cat is Not a Battleship: Thoughts on the Meaning of ‘Neuropsychoanalysis,’” and appreciate his focus on critical challenges that exist on a philosophical level in applying findings about the brain to psychoanalysis. As a practising psychoanalyst who has some interest in these matters, I appreciate, in particular, the non-antagonist tone of his contribution, a tone that has been unapologetically abandoned in similar contributions on the topic. Clarke invites dialogue and my comments are made in that spirit.

Clarke observes that subjectivity cannot be eliminated from our perspective on “objective” neuroscientific research:

… in coming to interpret and understand the findings of systematic research, any number of possible perspectives might be brought to bear, and the findings will be interpreted in any number of different ways. … objective study is framed by interpretive activity … (Clarke 2018, 440)

One such interpretation is that an unbridgeable gap exists, one that cannot be closed without making a “bloody mess” (Clarke 2018, 434). Another, as Clarke notes, is that as neuroscientific research becomes more sophisticated the gap cannot only be bridged but done away with—a “monadic” solution where brain findings are the final arbiters of subjective reality. Yet another interpretive stance is one of “loose juxtapositional toggling back and forth” where interpretation depends upon the concepts/phenomena being juxtaposed (Clarke 2018, 430). Our metaphors express our subjectivity: are we regarding one elephant or two … is the analyst the artist who need not know about the chemistry of the paint he uses (Blass and Carmeli 2007) or an art restorer (Sandberg 2016) where such knowledge can be helpful?

Clarke posits:

Only when we properly understand what a neurobiological explanation can and cannot explain, and when the differences between realms is clear, can the findings of neuroscience be evaluated as potentially relevant or not to psychoanalysis. (Clarke 2018, 436)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Travesía de lo corporal a lo simbólico corporal [Passage from the physical to the symbolic body]

by Eliana Rache, Buenos Aires/Mexico, Grupo Editorial LUMEN, 2015, 225 pp., ARS$ 300 (Paperback), ISBN 978-987-00-1067-8

José Fischbein

José Fischbein

Asociación Psicoanalítica Argentina

José Fischbein   jefischbein@gmail.com

In the book Passage from the Physical to the Symbolic Body, Dr Eliana Rache invites us to take part in an ongoing interrogation of different elements of our discipline. It is an enjoyable and profound text that takes us through the evolution and opening up of classical psychoanalysis, which has largely resulted from the current clinical demands of symptoms that go beyond psychoneurosis. The book places special attention on the processes by which the body makes itself present in the non-verbal, both in the act and directly in the imposition of the soma, with a focus on the non-verbal and the primitive sensory-motor aspects of communication that are present in verbal language.

Rache addresses the new conceptual instruments developed by diverse psychoanalysts who have engaged in this therapeutic field. The book focuses on narcissistic pathologies, approaches to these pathologies, and the need for changes to the classical psychoanalytic device in order to deal with the phenomena that occur in the transference-countertransference exchange, especially the openness to the psychic associativity of non-verbal languages that this exchange requires.

The author presents an ongoing dialogue between clinical work with severely ill patients and theories, both her own and those she selects from other authors, to provide a foundation and justification for the phenomena of the interpersonal transference-countertransference field. She demonstrates her profound knowledge of Freud's body of work, and she offers contributions based on her training in Kleinian theory, but she does not stop there; she begins her dialogical journey with post-Freudian developments. She frequently alludes to Ferenczi, Bion, and Winnicott, to then include the contributions of French analysts such as A. Green, Joyce MacDougall, Piera Aulagnier, as well as Sara and César Botella, culminating in a strong theoretical engagement with Roussillon that motivates, and saturates, a large part of her current vision. Her engagement with Roussillon is evident throughout the text. Often, the author's 627

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Beyond psychoanalytic literary criticism: Between literature and mind

1st edition, by Benjamin H. Ogden, London and New York, Routledge, 2018, 124pp., $34.37. ISBN: 978-0-8153-7728-3

Masha Mimran

Masha Mimran

New York
USA

Masha Mimran   mashamimran@gmail.com

Benjamin H. Ogden offers his reader a profoundly original take on dialogues between psychoanalysis and literature. An Assistant Professor in literature and humanities at Stevens Institute of Technology and also co-author, with Thomas H. Ogden, of The Analyst's Ear and the Critic's Eye: Rethinking Psychoanalysis and Literature (2013), Ogden plunges in this new book into the 817 depths and mesmerizing wonders of the power of interdisciplinarity in its purest form, far away from the mundane and often mechanically flavourless application of psychoanalytic readings of fiction and into the exciting, engaging, and highly sophisticated field that he poignantly creates before the reader's eye. It is an experiment of both mind and, dare I say, of the heart, capturing in the most spirited and vibrant ways how the meetings of psychoanalysis and literature can illuminate, recall, originate, and make alive again the meaning of both fields. With Beyond Psychoanalytic Literary Criticism: Between Literature and Mind, Ogden does indeed take his reader beyond psychoanalysis and literature, challenging us to what one might call a beneath of interdisciplinarity—where fiction, poetry, analytic thinking and psychoanalytic criticism converge in a starry constellation, making meaning arise anew. Ogden's reader, like an analysand, is given the Loewaldian opportunity for the rediscovery of old objects. It is this essential reawakening of psychoanalytic literary criticism that leaves Ogden's reader in uncanny territory, familiar with the intimate relationship between psychoanalysis and literature yet enthralled at every turn of the page by his innovative thinking, bridging the semantic spaces between the two fields. Psychoanalysts and literary scholars alike will find in Beyond Psychoanalytic Literary Criticism: Between Literature and Mind treasures of aesthetic passion and analytic thinking.

In the nine chapters offering readings of authors such as Vladimir Nabokov, Flannery O’Connor, J. M. Coetzee, and Elizabeth Bishop, Ogden himself demonstrates a poetic writing style which echoes his subjects and weaves the limits of psychoanalytic thinking into the fabric of literary criticism. He opens up the very task of the literary scholar who ought to be mindful of the mysterious inner workings of language. Ogden interprets narrative at the edges of potentiality, and like the psychoanalyst, inaugurates a space for meaning to unfold and not be foreclosed. Thus, Ogden begins our journey with his preface, writing that

the essays in this book constitute my effort over the past four years to explore new ways in which literature and psychoanalysis can be brought into conversation with one another, and to address, more broadly, the challenges and rewards of interdisciplinary writing and thinking. I have not gone about exploring these issues by presenting a single, sustained argument in support of any particular vision of how two fields must come together. I could not supply any such vision even if I had a desire to, for the act of writing itself is a kind of truth-seeking, and each time I write, the truth I am after is formed by the unique conditions and goals of that period of writing, and of that particular project. What is true in one chapter of this book is untrue in another, because the respective chapters are trying to achieve different ends, or meant to test the value of different lines of thinking. In that respect, this book is an experiment, as all life is an experiment. (xii)

responseSet.title: Le Hérisson [The Hedgehog] (2009). Director: Mona Achache
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Le Hérisson [The Hedgehog] (2009). Director: Mona Achache

Michel Granek

Michel Granek

Israel

Michel Granek   CONTACT Michel Granek michelgranek@gmail.com Hapardes 21, Rishpon 4691500, Israel

Le hérisson [The Hedgehog]

One often hears a conversation of this sort: “Have you read Anna Karenina?” (Tolstoy 1952 [1878]) “No, but I saw the movie,” or in this case, “Have you read The Elegance of the Hedgehog?” “No, but I saw the movie.” The response is as absurd as it appears natural; at least, it avoids the question of the connections between the book and the film, leaving the impression that the film is equivalent to the book, something that all evidence would suggest is erroneous. Generally speaking, the problematic of the relationship between a book and a film that is adapted from it is as old as cinema itself, so I will only underscore two points. Firstly, a book is entirely capable of expressing the characters’ flow of consciousness and their associations, whereas this is more challenging for a film and demands a certain level of sophistication on the part of the director. Consequently, a film has more of a tendency to emphasize the narrative and the frame. The second point is that the characters, décor etc. that appear on screen are not necessarily compatible with those imagined by a viewer who had also read the novel, an incompatibility that may have the effect of making it difficult for the viewer to fully enter into the aesthetic illusion (Kris 1952). The question is particularly pertinent in considering the connections between the novel The Elegance of the Hedgehog and the film Le hérisson, which I will come back to.

The film, released in 2009, is the first full-length work by the director Mona Achache. It is a free adaptation of the book by philosopher and author Muriel Barbery, published in 2006 (Barbery 2006). The director understood that the success of the book—an extraordinary success, as the book had won five literary prizes and that same year was translated into 34 languages—carried the film, and she had reason to fear the reaction of viewers who had read the book. Le hérisson, however, is her film, and more specifically her point of view about the book. The film itself was awarded the Washington International Film Festival (2011) prize and five further nominations.
