responseSet.title: Intervention into an institute at a crossroads: A methodology and rationale
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intervention into an institute at a crossroads: A methodology and rationale

John Lundgren

John Lundgren

Psychoanalytic Center of CA, Los Angeles, California, USA

David Geffen School of Medicine, UCLA, Los Angeles, California, USA

A. K. Rice Institute, GREX, Los Angeles, California, USA

John Lundgren   CONTACT John Lundgren jlundgre@ucla.edu GREX, 9777 Wilshire Blvd., #905, Beverly Hills, CA 90212, USA

Bion, group relations, social defenses, action research

This case study reports on the creation of a consulting intervention for a psychoanalytic institute that had reached a developmental crossroads. There was difficulty in filling leadership positions within the organization, and the operation of cliques and factions had produced a general atmosphere of fear and hostility leading to institutional apathy. An internal organization consultation was established and negotiated an intervention by way of a “self study.” The intervention was mediated by way of an extensive confidential survey designed to explore overt and covert issues and dynamics. Data analysis generated a report that formed the basis for a series of face-to-face institute-wide meetings to “digest” the data, formulate collective interpretations and evolve action plans for implementation of changes. This case study includes a seven-year follow-up since the initial intervention. The social dynamics of psychoanalytic institutes will be explored with focus on the dynamic influence of the pair culture and social systems as defences against collective anxieties. Finally, implications for future interventions into institutes, including the concept of analysis of the “group self” of members and training candidates, will be discussed.

Cette étude de cas porte sur la création d'une expertise-conseil au sein d'un institut psychanalytique ayant atteint un point critique dans son développement.

Le leadership n'était plus assuré au sein de l'organisation, qui était aux mains de cliques et de factions. Cela avait produit une atmosphère de peur et d'hostilité généralisée et conduit à une apathie institutionnelle. Une consultation-conseil interne à l'organisation fut mise en place et aboutit à une intervention basée sur une « self study » (étude introspective).

L'intervention fut médiatisée via une enquête confidentielle approfondie destinée à explorer la dynamique et les questions manifestes et latentes. L'analyse des données donna lieu à un rapport qui forma la base de toute une série de réunions institutionnelles pour « digérer » les données, formuler des interprétations collectives et proposer des plans d'action pour la mise en œuvre de changements. Cette étude de cas inclut un suivi sur sept ans depuis l'intervention initiale. L'étude de la dynamique sociale des instituts de psychanalyse mettra l'accent sur l'influence dynamique de la culture des pairs et des systèmes sociaux comme défenses contre les angoisses collectives. Enfin, la discussion portera sur les conséquences de futures interventions au sein des instituts, y compris le concept de l'analyse du « self de groupe » des membres et des candidats en formation.

Dieser Fallbericht handelt von der beratenden Intervention für ein psychoanalytisches Institut, das in seiner Entwicklung an einem Scheideweg angelangt war. Es gab Schwierigkeiten, Führungspositionen in der Organisation zu besetzen, und die Aktivität von Cliquen und Gruppen hatte eine allgemeine Atmosphäre der Angst und Feindseligkeit erzeugt, die die Institution apathisch machte. Man gründete eine interne Organisationsberatung und führte eine Intervention mittels „Selbsterforschung“ [self study] durch. Am Beginn der Intervention stand ein umfangreicher, vertraulich behandelter Fragebogen, der auf offene und verdeckte Schwierigkeiten und Dynamiken abhob. Die Datenanalyse ermöglichte einen Bericht, der als Grundlage einer Reihe von Sitzungen diente, an denen sämtliche Institutsmitglieder teilnahmen, um die Daten zu „verdauen“, gemeinsam Deutungen zu formulieren und verändernde Maßnahmen zu planen. Diese Fallstudie umfasst auch ein Follow-up, das sieben Jahre nach der Erstintervention durchgeführt wurde. Die soziale Dynamik psychoanalytischer Institute wird mit Blick auf den dynamischen Einfluss der Paarkultur und der sozialen Systeme als Abwehr kollektiver Ängste untersucht. Abschließend werden Implikationen für künftige Interventionen in Instituten einschließlich des Konzepts der Analyse des „Gruppenselbst“ der Mitglieder und Ausbildungskandidaten erörtert.

L’articolo descrive il processo attraverso cui è stata pensata e si è poi svolta una consultazione per facilitare l’intervento in un istituto psicoanalitico che aveva raggiunto un bivio nel suo percorso di sviluppo. Nell’istituto si erano manifestate delle difficoltà nell’assegnazione di posizioni di leadership, e la presenza attiva di sottogruppi e fazioni aveva creato un’atmosfera generale di paura e ostilità che stava portando a un clima di apatia istituzionale. Si è allora deciso di procedere a una consultazione interna all’organizzazione, in seguito alla quale è stato negoziato un intervento basato su uno “studio di sè” da parte dell’istituto stesso. L’intervento è stato mediato facendo riferimento a un ampio sondaggio confidenziale concepito apposta per esplorare aspetti e dinamiche sia visibili sia nascosti. A partire dall’analisi dei dati è stato stilato un rapporto che è servito poi da punto di partenza e riferimento per una serie di incontri faccia a faccia con membri dell’intero istituto: incontri che avevano la finalità di “digerire” i dati, di formulare interpretazioni collettive e di sviluppare piani d’azione per l’implementazione di cambiamenti. Le dinamiche sociali degli istituti psicoanalitici verranno esplorate in queste pagine prestando particolare attenzione all’influenza dinamica della cultura e dei sistemi sociali laddove vengono utilizzati come difese contro angosce collettive. In ultimo verranno discusse le implicazioni per futuri interventi in altri istituti, e tra queste il concetto di analisi del “Sé di gruppo” dei membri e dei candidati in training.

Este estudio de caso aborda la creación de una intervención de consulta para un instituto psicoanalítico en una encrucijada en su desarrollo. Había dificultad en ocupar posiciones de liderazgo dentro de la organización, y las operaciones de camarillas y facciones habían producido una atmósfera general de temor y hostilidad, lo que conducía a la apatía institucional. Se estableció una consulta de organización interna y se negoció una intervención bajo la forma de un “estudio del self”. La intervención estuvo mediada por una encuesta confidencial extensa, diseñada para explorar cuestiones y dinámicas tanto abiertas como encubiertas. El análisis de datos generó un informe que constituyó la base para una serie de reuniones presenciales de todo el instituto a fin de “digerir” los datos, formular interpretaciones colectivas y desarrollar planes de acción para la implementación de cambios. Este estudio de caso incluye un seguimiento de siete años, desde la intervención inicial. Se explora la dinámica social del instituto psicoanalítico, centrándose en la influencia dinámica del par cultura y sistemas sociales como defensas contra angustias colectivas. Finalmente, se discuten las implicancias para intervenciones futuras en institutos, incluido el concepto analítico de “self grupal” de los miembros y candidatos en formación.

responseSet.title: Play in the analytic setting: The development and communication of meaning in child analysis
responseSet.newSectionName: The Development of Meaning in Child and Adolescent Analysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Play in the analytic setting: The development and communication of meaning in child analysis

Judith Yanof

Judith Yanof

W. Newton
MA
USA

Judith Yanof   CONTACT Judith A. Yanof jyanof@erols.com 25 Somerset Road, W. Newton, MA, 02465, USA
play, therapeutic action, play technique, play state, child analysis

Child analysts increasingly appreciate that play can stand on its own as a mutative process. When the analyst shares a child’s “play state,” her participation creates a transitional space in which a therapeutic process is enhanced. During play, children experiment with new, improvisational ways of sharing and negotiating meaning between themselves and another. The child and analyst co-create a new lived-experience that can be organized and integrated into a different narrative about the child and his world. This paper demonstrates, through a clinical illustration, how the analyst’s participation in play works therapeutically.

Les analystes d'enfants apprécient de plus en plus que le jeu peut être un processus de transformation sui generis. Lorsque l'analyste partage ‘l'état de jeu’ d'un enfant, sa participation crée un espace transitionnel qui avance le processus thérapeutique. En jouant, les enfants tentent de nouvelles manières improvisées de partager et négocier un sens entre eux-mêmes et un autre. L'enfant et l'analyste co-créent ensemble une nouvelle expérience vécue qui peut être organisée et intégrée dans un récit différent au sujet de l'enfant et de son monde. L’auteur démontre, à travers une illustration clinique, comment la participation de l'analyste au jeu fonctionne thérapeutiquement.

Kinderanalytiker wissen immer mehr zu schätzen, dass Spiel für sich allein als mutativer Prozess stehen kann. Ist die Analytikerin am „Spielzustand“ des Kindes beteiligt, so schafft ihre Teilnahme einen Übergangsraum, in dem ein therapeutischer Prozess gefördert wird. Während des Spiels experimentieren Kinder mit neuen, improvisierten Mitteln, um Bedeutung mit einer anderen Person zu teilen und sie dieser Person zu vermitteln. Das Kind und die Analytikerin erschaffen gemeinsam eine neue gelebte Erfahrung, die in eine andere Narrative über das Kind und dessen Welt eingeordnet und integriert werden kann. Dieser Beitrag veranschaulicht anhand eines klinischen Beispiels, wie die Teilnahme der Analytikerin am Spiel therapeutisch wirkt.

Che il gioco rappresenti di per sé un processo di natura mutativa è un fatto sempre più riconosciuto da parte degli psicoanalisti dell’infanzia. Quando l’analista prende parte allo “stato di gioco” del bambino, la sua partecipazione crea uno spazio transizionale all’interno del quale il processo terapeutico risulta potenziato. Durante il gioco i bambini sperimentano modi nuovi e improvvisati di condividere e negoziare il significato con un’altra persona. Il bambino e l’analista co-creano una nuova esperienza vissuta che può venire organizzata e integrata in una narrazione diversa sul bambino e sul suo mondo. L’articolo mostra con una vignetta clinica in che modo la partecipazione dell’analista al gioco funziona in senso terapeutico.

Los analistas de niños reconocen cada vez más que el juego en sí mismo es un proceso mutativo. Cuando un analista comparte el “estado de juego” de un niño, dicha participación crea un espacio transicional en el que el proceso terapéutico se intensifica. Durante el juego, los niños experimentan con formas improvisadas y nuevas de compartir y negociar significados entre ellos mismos y con otros. El niño y el analista co-crean una nueva experiencia vivida, que puede organizarse e integrarse en una narrativa diferente sobre el niño y su mundo. Mediante un ejemplo clínico, se muestra cómo la participación del analista en el juego funciona terapéuticamente.

responseSet.title: The proleptic unconscious and the exemplary moment in psychoanalysis
responseSet.newSectionName: The Unconsciousness of Mental Life: Where do we locate the unconscious? Where and how do we find it in the session? How do we speak to it? What are the necessary conditions of the setting?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The proleptic unconscious and the exemplary moment in psychoanalysis

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

San Francisco
CA
USA

Mitchell Wilson   CONTACT Mitchell Wilson mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu 2960A Piedmont Ave., Berkeley, CA 94705, USA

In this paper I argue that a central feature of manifestations of the unconscious in clinical work is its proleptic dimension. In its essence, the proleptic unconscious is anticipatory and futural in its orientation. The classical view of unconscious mental life posits the existence of a spacial location filled with positive content there to be discovered; but this view risks reification and a clinical approach in which the analyst “finds what she is looking for.” By contrast, the propleptic unconscious is without content; it is the to-be-realized. Such realizations at times take on an exemplary function. Exemplification points toward future uses rather than an enduring set of underlying principles. Thus, the subject is interpolated by realizations of the proleptic unconscious toward future possibilities and emerging ways of being. Analysts within the American ego psychological tradition, whose work grappled with the problem of reification of unconscious content, anticipated the proleptic unconscious. Clinical examples are discussed.

L'auteur soutient qu'une propriété essentielle des manifestations de l'inconscient dans le travail clinique en est la dimension proleptique. Essentiellement, l'inconscient proleptique est anticipatoire et orienté vers le futur. La vision classique de l'inconscient conjecture l'existence d'un lieu spatial rempli de contenu palpable à découvrir; mais ce point de vue risque la réification d'une approche clinique où l'analyste trouve ce qu'il recherche. En revanche, l'inconscient proleptique est sans contenu; c'est le à-réaliser. De telles réalisations prennent parfois une dimension exemplaire. L'exemplification pointe vers des utilisations futures plutôt qu'un ensemble durable de principes sous-jacents. Le sujet se trouve ainsi interpolé par les réalisations de l'inconscient proleptique vers des possibilités futures et des façons émergentes d'être. Les analystes de la tradition américaine de la psychologie du Moi, dont le travail s'est accroché au problème de la réification du contenu inconscient, ont anticipé l'inconscient proleptique. Suivent des exemples cliniques.

Ich argumentiere in diesem Beitrag, dass ein zentrales Merkmal von Manifestationen des Unbewussten in der klinischen Arbeit ihre proleptische Dimension ist. In seinem Wesen ist das proleptische Unbewusste vorwegnehmend und auf die Zukunft ausgerichtet. Die traditionelle Sicht des unbewussten geistigen Lebens postuliert die Existenz eines räumlichen Ortes, der mit positivem Inhalt gefüllt ist, den es zu entdecken gilt; aber diese Sicht riskiert eine Reifikation und einen klinischen Ansatz, bei dem die Analytikerin „findet, was sie sucht“. Im Gegensatz dazu ist das proleptische Unbewusste ohne Inhalt; es ist das zu Realisierende. Solche Realisierungen haben zuweilen eine beispielhafte Funktion. Exemplifizierung weist den Weg zur künftigen Verwendung, anstatt auf ein fortdauerndes zugrunde liegendes Regelwerk zu verweisen. Auf diese Weise wird das Subjekt durch Realisierungen des proleptischen Unbewussten in die Richtung künftiger Möglichkeiten und neu in Erscheinung tretender Formen des Seins interpoliert. Analytiker in der Tradition der US-amerikanischen Ich-Psychologie, deren Arbeit mit dem Problem der Reifikation unbewusster Inhalte gerungen hat, antizipierten das proleptische Unbewusste. Es werden klinische Beispiele erörtert.

In questo articolo sostengo che una caratteristica centrale delle manifestazioni dell'inconscio nel lavoro clinico è costituita dalla sua dimensione prolettica. Essenzialmente, l'inconscio prolettico ha una natura anticipatoria ed è orientato nel senso del futuro. Il modo classico di guardare alla vita mentale inconscia presuppone l'esistenza di uno spazio riempito di contenuti veri e propri che devono essere scoperti: il problema è che questa prospettiva rischia da un lato di essere reificante e dall'altro di condurre a un approccio clinico in cui l'analista semplicemente “trova quello che sta cercando”. L'inconscio prolettico, invece, è privo di contenuti: esso consiste in qualcosa che deve sempre e ancora essere realizzato. Questo genere di realizzazione assume talvolta una funzione esemplare, e l'esemplificazione suggerisce usi futuri piuttosto che un insieme costante nel tempo di principi sottostanti. Per questo si può dire che nel soggetto hanno luogo delle realizzazioni dell'inconscio prolettico che rinviano a possibilità future e a modalità di esistenza man mano affioranti. Gli analisti appartenenti alla tradizione americana della Psicologia dell'Io, confrontatisi a lungo nel loro lavoro con il problema della reificazione dei contenuti inconsci, hanno anticipato nel loro pensiero l'idea dell'inconscio prolettico. Nell'articolo vengono anche discussi alcuni casi clinici.

En este artículo se argumenta que un rasgo central de las manifestaciones del inconsciente en el trabajo clínico es su dimensión proléptica. El inconsciente proléptico es, en esencia, anticipatorio y orientado hacia el futuro. La perspectiva clásica de la vida mental inconsciente plantea la existencia de un espacio lleno de contenido positivo a ser descubierto; pero esta perspectiva lleva el riesgo de una cosificación y un enfoque clínico en el que los analistas “encuentran lo que están buscando”. En cambio, el inconsciente proléptico no tiene contenido; es lo que va a materializarse. Estas materializaciones a veces cumplen una función ejemplar. La ejemplificación apunta hacia usos futuros, en vez de hacerlo hacia un conjunto duradero de principios subyacentes. Así, el sujeto es interpolado por materializaciones del inconsciente proléptico hacia futuras posibilidades y formas emergentes de ser. Los analistas dentro de la tradición de la psicología del yo estadounidense, cuyo trabajo trata de resolver el problema de la cosificación del contenido inconsciente, anticiparon el inconsciente proléptico. Se analizan ejemplos clínicos.

responseSet.title: Plural feminine: Hysteria, masochism or melancholia?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Plural feminine: Hysteria, masochism or melancholia?**

Catherine Chabert

Catherine Chabert

Catherine Chabert   CONTACT Catherine Chabert Catherine@chabert.org 76, Rue Charlot, 75003 Paris, France

Hanna, a Figure of Masochism

She was 30 years old but looked like a little girl, awkward, hampered by a body with no curves, wrapped in loose and colourless clothing. She saw herself as futureless, her professional life was in a rut, her marriage was petering out in the violence of words and blows. She was overrun by pernicious and obsessive anxiety which she drowned in alcohol—the fear of being a “bad mother.”

As the eldest daughter, she thought she had failed to satisfy her mother who was ostensibly delighted about the birth of three boys, robust little fellows who took up all the space because of the vitality of their will and the brilliance of their success. Lost in this manly crowd, Hanna hung on to an absent and exciting father, jealously guarded by his seductive and sensual wife. However, whenever Hanna felt desperate or threatened, he was always there: if anyone was to save her, it was him!

Hanna used her sessions like irremovable landmarks, like a space of infinite grievances colonised by relentless self-incrimination. She seemed very attached to her daily misery and even her dreams revealed themselves consistent with the destitute reality of her life—dark, sticky, muddy: the expression, if not the ostentatious display, of a pervasive form of masochism made up the leading thread of the transference.

Beyond that murky world, she would let out, as if by accident, small glimmers that she put out very quickly but which led me to think that, hiding within her, were a few more gems whose sparkle she persisted in obscuring: she would furtively mention the brilliant life of her family because of her father’s high-level post, the sumptuous places where she spent her childhood, a few loving and ephemeral childminders. An unknown or masked princess, she reminded me of a dreamless Cinderella. But she had no use for a fairy godmother: no magic in language, no impact of my words, my constructions or my interpretations. I had to remain still, silent, so that she may carry on with her monologue, the long unfolding of her sorrows, without any pause, any sigh, any grand finale. In short, I had to maintain, on the altar of the transference, the mausoleum of her masochism and melancholia: a “dead mother,”1 this is what I had to be, a mother who is impervious to grievances and claims, a mute, unavailable mother and, most importantly, a mother who did not look at her.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to the Exhibition in Honour of the Centenary of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen

London
UK

Dana Birksted-Breen   CONTACT Dana Birksted-Breen dana.birksted-breen@dib.me.uk Stables Lodge, 7a Ellerdale Road, London NW3 6BA

Like the artist, the analyst tries to take possession of the word again by freeing it from the suffocation of everyday use; he searches for the echoes of the very elements of every language and of every experience, fully aware of the inevitable disparity between what his formulation will be able to express and all the rest that remains unsaid and that perhaps cannot be either said or heard. (Canestri 1994, 1090)

We travel far in thought, in imagination or in the realm of memory. Events happened as they happened, not all of them of course but here and there a memory or a fragment of a dream-picture is actual, is real, is like a work of art or is a work of art … there are priceless broken fragments that are meaningless until we find the other broken bits to match them.

(Doolittle, poet, describing her analysis with Freud 1971, 41–42)

There is a long history of interchange between psychoanalysis and art. In the 1950s, Marion Milner, researching into free-floating states of mind for self-exploration in the analytic setting and in artistic creativity, writes:

a strong case could be made out … for seeing the basic function of a work or art, not as communication (though it can serve as that), not as reparation (though it can stand for that), not as giving reassurance that there is a sphere in which conflict can be solved (though it does do that); but as having to do essentially with the very basis of our interest in anything beyond the primary objects of instincts. In fact, it not only uses symbols, it creates symbols. By means of its subtle fusion of form and content, feeling and idea, it reveals the familiar in the unfamiliar, and so brings about that extension and transference of interest which Ernest Jones describes as the basis of civilization. (Milner 1954, 411)

Sixty years later, using the Bionian notion of reverie, sculptor and psychoanalyst Shierry Weber Nicholsen discusses the role of reverie both in his stone work and in the analytic work:

I have this piece of stone, and I turn myself to it, so to speak. I contemplate it, moving around it with my eyes and also turning it, on the lookout for some starting point – you could say, for some initial point of contact between my mind (or my psyche, I’ll use the word mind in the broad sense here) and the stone. In other words, I try to put my mind in a state of reverie while I look at the stone and I hope that something will emerge. It might be some time, sometimes even months, before this initial contact is made (and the same thing can happen of course with a prospective or a new patient). In the meantime, I am turning the stone over in my gaze from time to time, not necessarily looking hard at any one spot, rather just letting its various aspects pass by my gaze and probably my touch as well, a little like the way Freud talks of free association as being like the passenger in the train just looking out the window at what goes by.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter from Madrid: The beginning of psychoanalysis in Spain

María Muñoz de la Cruz and Teresa Olmos de Paz

Teresa Olmos de Paz

Teresa Olmos de Paz   teresaolmoslp@gmail.com

Psychoanalysis owes its arrival in Spain to the early translation of Sigmund Freud's works and the scientific relationship between Spanish psychiatrists and German and French psychiatry in the early twentieth century.

In 1917, at the suggestion of Spanish philosopher José Ortega y Gasset, Ruiz Castillo, the director of the Biblioteca Nueva publishing house, acquired the rights to publish all of Freud's current and future work. He commissioned Luis López Ballesteros for the translation, whom Freud himself lauded for his “very correct interpretation” of his thoughts. In fact, the translation of Sigmund Freud's texts into Spanish was the first translation of his work into a foreign language, in what constitutes the greatest contribution to the diffusion of psychoanalysis in the Spanish language.

The ongoing publication of Freud's work led to its diffusion among Spanish-speaking scientific, intellectual, academic and artistic circles. A group of artists and writers at the Student Residence (Residencia de Estudiantes) even extended an invitation to Freud to visit Spain to deliver a series of lectures, but illness ultimately prevented this project from coming to fruition.

However, in October 1928, Sandor Ferenczi travelled to Spain and delivered a talk at the Student Residence. Years later, Dali and Picasso would be among the writers and artists who, led by Thomas Mann, organised a tribute to Freud on his 80th birthday. Dali visited Freud again during his exile in London, where he painted his portrait.

One of the first Spanish psychiatrists interested in psychoanalysis was Ángel Garma, who could be considered Spain's first psychoanalyst. He began his psychoanalytic training in 1927 at the Berlin Psychoanalytic Institute, and he was admitted as a member of the German Psychoanalytic Society in 1931. In November of that year, he returned to Madrid and began his work as a psychoanalyst, but soon after, in 1936, the Spanish Civil War would break out, thwarting his plans to establish a psychoanalytic group. Ángel Garma eventually settled in Argentina, and in December 1942, he and a group of pioneers founded the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Precious little: Birth and death in the analytic process

Patrick Miller

Patrick Miller

Paris
France

Patrick Miller   CONTACT Patrick Miller patmil75@gmail.com 21 Boulevard Richard Lenoir, 75011, Paris, France

What makes for the liveliness of a silence? How does liveliness prevail over destructivity in the unconscious encounter between two body-minds in the analytic process? What is being experienced unconsciously can go against the tendency to repeat, because the unconscious and conscious response of the analyst does not repeat the closing and/or the intrusiveness of the primary environment. The necessary receptivity required from the analyst and his/her unconscious capacity to accept an active passivation and a state of relative selflessness supposes that his/her narcissistic defences can be suspended to allow for working through rather than acting out. The unconsciousness of the analyst’s mental life can be seen as a safeguard to the liveliness of the process. Just as his unconscious defences can sometimes hinder the ongoing development of the analytic process and elaboration. Interpretation only comes as an end result of a sustained contact and intercourse of the analyst’s hopefully lively unconscious mental life and that of the analysand. Overcoming the risk that excitation prevails over tenderness is part and parcel of the constant unconscious working through of the analyst. The acting out works as a soul murder and tends to destroy the potential fecundity of the analytic encounter.

D’où provient la vivacité d'un silence ? Comment la vivacité l'emporte-t-elle sur la destructivité dans la rencontre inconsciente entre deux esprits-corps dans le processus analytique ? Le vécu inconscient peut aller à l'encontre de la tendance à la répétition, car la réponse inconsciente et consciente de l'analyste ne répète pas le rejet et/ou l'intrusion de l'environnement primaire. La réceptivité nécessaire de la part de l'analyste et sa capacité inconsciente à accepter une passivation active et un état relatif d'altruisme suppose que ses défenses narcissiques peuvent être suspendues pour permettre la perlaboration plutôt qu’une mise en acte. L'inconscience de la vie mentale de l'analyste peut être considérée comme une garantie de la vivacité du processus, tout comme les défenses inconscientes peuvent parfois entraver le développement continu du processus analytique et son élaboration. L'interprétation n'est que le résultat d'un contact et d'un dialogue entre l’inconscient bonnement vivace de l’analyste et de celui de l'analysand. Surmonter le risque que l'excitation prévaut sur la tendresse fait partie intégrante de la perlaboration constante de l'analyste. La mise en acte fonctionne comme meurtre d’âme et tend à détruire la fécondité potentielle de la rencontre analytique.

Was macht eine Stille lebendig? Wie setzt sich Lebendigkeit in der unbewussten Begegnung von zweimal Körper und Geist im analytischen Prozess gegen Destruktivität durch? Was unbewusst erlebt wird, kann im Widerspruch zur Wiederholungstendenz stehen, denn die unbewusste und bewusste Reaktion des Analytikers wiederholt nicht das Ausschließende und/oder Zudringliche der Primärumgebung. Die von dem Analytiker/der Analytikerin benötigte notwendige Aufnahmebereitschaft und seine/ihre unbewusste Fähigkeit, eine aktive Passivierung und einen Zustand relativer Selbstlosigkeit zu akzeptieren, setzt voraus, dass seine/ihre narzisstischen Abwehrmechanismen aufgehoben werden können, um ein Durcharbeiten statt eines Ausagierens zu erreichen. Man kann die Unbewusstheit des geistigen Lebens des Analytikers als Schutz der Lebendigkeit des Prozesses sehen. Genau wie dessen unbewusste Abwehr manchmal die laufende Entwicklung des analytischen Prozesses und die Ausarbeitung behindern kann. Die Deutung kommt erst als abschließendes Ergebnis eines ausdauernden Kontakts und der Wechselbeziehung zwischen dem hoffentlich lebendigen unbewussten geistigen Lebens des Analytikers und das des Analysanden. Die Überwindung des Risikos, dass sich Erregung gegen Güte durchsetzt, ist untrennbar mit dem ständigen unbewussten Durcharbeiten des Analytikers verbunden.

Che cosa può far sì che un silenzio sia pieno di vita? E come può la vitalità prevalere sulla distruttività nell’incontro inconscio tra due corpi-menti nel contesto del processo analitico? Ciò che viene esperito inconsciamente può contrastare la tendenza alla ripetizione nella misura in cui la risposta inconscia e conscia dell’analista non riproduce la chiusura e/o l’intrusività dell’ambiente primario. La necessaria recettività richiesta all’analista, insieme alla sua capacità inconscia di accettare uno stato di attiva passivizzazione e di relativo altruismo, presuppongono che le sue difese narcisistiche si possano temporaneamente disattivare in modo da favorire un’attività di elaborazione psichica anziché il ricorso all’agito. La parte inconscia della vita mentale dell’analista può essere vista in questo senso come fattore di protezione della vitalità del processo, proprio come in altri casi le sue difese inconsce possono rappresentare viceversa un ostacolo per lo sviluppo del processo analitico e dell’elaborazione. L’interpretazione arriva soltanto come tappa finale di un contatto e di un rapporto sostenuti nel tempo tra la vita mentale inconscia (e sperabilmente vitale) dell’analista e quella dell’analizzando. Evitare di volta in volta il rischio che l’eccitamento prevalga sulla tenerezza è parte integrante del constante lavoro di elaborazione inconscia dell’analista. L’agito funziona invece come un assassinio d’anima, e tende a distruggere la potenziale fecondità dell’incontro analitico.

¿Qué hace que un silencio sea vivaz? ¿Cómo prevalece la vivacidad sobre la destructividad en el encuentro inconsciente entre dos cuerpos-mentes en el proceso analítico? Lo que se experimenta inconscientemente puede ir en contra de la tendencia a repetir, debido a que la respuesta conciente e inconsciente del analista no repite la cerrazón y/o el carácter intrusivo del entorno primario. La receptividad necesaria requerida del analista y su capacidad inconsciente para aceptar una “pasivación” activa y un estado de relativo desapego de sí mismo supone que sus defensas narcisistas pueden suspenderse para permitir la elaboración, en vez del acting-out. Se puede considerar la inconsciencia de la vida mental del analista como una salvaguardia de la vivacidad del proceso, tal como sus defensas inconscientes pueden a veces entrabar el desarrollo del proceso analítico y la elaboración. La interpretación solo llega como resultado final del sostenido contacto e intimidad de la vida mental inconsciente esperanzadamente vivaz del analista y la el analizado. Superar el riesgo de que prevalezca la excitación sobre la ternura es parte integral de la constante elaboración inconsciente del analista. La actuación funciona como un asesinato del alma y tiende a destruir la potencial fecundidad del encuentro analítico.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

100 years of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen   dana.birksted-breen@dib.me.uk

Unperturbed by a letter from a London publisher (1924), saying that the latest news from America suggested that “psycho-analysis was in decline already, the subject having suddenly gone ‘flop’”, Dr. Jones set the course by which the International Journal and the Library became highly respected means of communication and worthy archives of psycho-analytic development. (Payne 1958, 308)

Although the idea of an English language journal had first been mentioned in 1913, it was not until after World War I that the idea came to fruition. Jones tells Freud that the time is ripe for an English language journal in the same letter in which he mentions his grief for the recent death of his young wife. Coming not long after the devastation of the First World War, the new journal becomes a sign of survival and of life over death, of creativity and action in the wake of mourning.

You see I can look forward in life, although I have been through hell itself these last three months. It has been an indescribably terrible experience, signifying more even than a tremendous loss—owing to my inner psychical situation and the poignant circumstances of my wife’s death. But I am surely winning through, and have learnt very much by it. (Jones 1918, 326)

Freud replies on 22 December 2018: “Needless to say we are all impatient to get your contributions to the Zeitschrift and see you taking an active part in the new career, opening for ‘her’.”

Different agendas, personal rivalries and financial issues were at stake. Freud writes to Jones in January 1919:

As for the journal, I hope you know, that both periodicals, Zeitschrift and Imago, have become official with the “European” groups. I hope also you have heard by Sachs, that we are setting on foot a ψα Verlag. Now Rank developed the idea at the same time as you, that you should bring out an English edition of the Zeitschrift (or both), all the papers getting translated into the two languages.

Meanwhile Abraham wanted to resuscitate the Jahrbuch, which had been the first of a number of psychoanalytic journals started at the turn of the century, this one under the direction of Jung, and which had folded due to rivalries and disputes, and economic problems. But Freud warns:

 …  a third publication would be restricted to the purely Ψα public, which does not have much buying power. [ … ] I am afraid that the material produced by us in the course of a year might not be sufficient to keep it going. We do not exactly have an abundance of material even for the Zeitschrift [ … ] what England (and America) produce is henceforward to be diverted to the English Journal of Ψα. (Freud 1919a, 401)
